concepts.description: Redeven builds a deterministic embedded OKF bundle from source Markdown concepts.

frontmatter.description: Redeven builds a deterministic embedded OKF bundle from source Markdown concepts.
frontmatter.title: OKF bundle lifecycle

concepts.summary:
Redeven builds its embedded OKF bundle from curated source files under the top-level `okf/` authoring root, verifies deterministic dist outputs under `okf/dist/`, and embeds the resulting bundle into the binary used by runtime and AI features.

Redeven builds a deterministic embedded OKF bundle from source Markdown concepts.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

`build_assets.sh` invokes `build_okf_bundle.sh`, which runs `cmd/okf-bundle` against explicit source and dist roots. The builder scans OKF Markdown concepts, parses Summary, Contract, Boundaries, section metadata, and structured Evidence, skips generated `dist/` while hashing the source tree into `source_sha256`, and writes the bundle, manifest, and checksum outputs. The schema 3 manifest records concept, section, and evidence counts.

Authoring quality uses the same parser and builder rather than a second Markdown implementation. `check_content_quality.sh --report-only` reports legacy layout, index coverage, summary budgets, oversized contracts, evidence problems, and duplicate paragraphs without blocking migration. `--strict` turns structural, indexing, invalid evidence, duplicate large-paragraph, and over-budget violations into failures. The final integration gate runs strict quality after source integrity and before verifying the generated bundle.

Each source concept is a retrieval unit with Summary, Contract, Boundaries, and Evidence. Summary and section bodies are embedded as structured data; Evidence remains in the same source file but is omitted from ordinary concept opening unless explicitly requested. `okf/dist/*` is regenerated from the source tree and remains committed verification output rather than an authoring surface.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body: The bundle remains auditable only while OKF Markdown concepts are the sole authoring surface, quality rules are enforced through the shared parser, and `okf/dist/*` stays a generated verification artifact set rather than hand-edited truth. A `quality_exception` may document a justified cross-domain length exception, but it does not disable structural, evidence, indexing, or duplicate-content checks.

concepts.title: OKF tool suite
concepts.description: OKF tools expose read-only Redeven repository knowledge through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.

frontmatter.description: OKF tools expose read-only Redeven repository knowledge through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.
frontmatter.title: OKF tool suite

concepts.summary:
Redeven exposes the embedded OKF bundle through read-only `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open` builtin tools, and the shared Flower surface renders OKF activity as project knowledge lookup rows with expandable detail.

OKF tools expose read-only Redeven repository knowledge through index browsing, short search, and concept opening.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Runtime OKF code loads the embedded bundle once, indexes root directory sections, scores structured summaries and sections in memory, and opens a concept summary or one selected section by id or path. The bundle builder parses internal Markdown links into deterministic `links` and `backlinks` and parses Evidence into structured references. Builtin tool registration advertises OKF tools as non-mutating local-read tools with structured presentation metadata, tool execution dispatches behind a read-permission gate, and Flower activity rows render compact knowledge-lookup details. OKF activity labels and operation values come from `ToolPresentationSpec`, so the Floret projection does not own a separate OKF display vocabulary.

## Tool workflow

`okf.index` browses `okf/index.md` as a structured directory and returns summaries plus section counts. `okf.search` returns a bounded short candidate list with query-aware snippets and the best matching section id/title; title and Summary matches outrank section body matches. Broad searches should start with the default small result count, and `has_more` means additional concepts exist rather than content truncation.

`okf.open` without `section` returns the concise Summary and section catalog. A second open with a section id returns only that section and applies the body window within the selected section. Evidence is omitted by default and is returned only through `section=evidence` or `include_evidence=true`. Models should follow `okf.index -> okf.search -> okf.open Summary -> okf.open section` and must not rely on snippets for detailed contracts.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body: The tools return scoped Redeven repository knowledge and do not mutate OKF source or dist artifacts. They do not access the internet and are not a substitute for web search, direct URL fetching, or external source validation. They must not be used for current news, recent public events, market or pricing facts, third-party documentation, external facts, or general web facts. Source-level conclusions still require file or terminal verification after OKF navigation.

concepts.title: Redeven environment operations
concepts.description: redeven env and redeven targets exec are the product boundaries for Flower and automation environment lifecycle and target diagnostic requests.

frontmatter.description: redeven env and redeven targets exec are the product boundaries for Flower and automation environment lifecycle and target diagnostic requests.
frontmatter.title: Redeven environment operations

concepts.summary: Redeven environment lifecycle requests must go through the Redeven product surface, not through inferred container, SSH, systemd, launchctl, or process-manager commands. Flower learns this boundary through the standard `redeven-environment` system skill, not through a bespoke environment workflow embedded in the global prompt.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The `redeven env` CLI surface exposes machine-readable environment status, diagnostics, and lifecycle operation plans through the shared agent protocol envelope. `list`, `resolve`, `status`, `diagnose`, and `stop` are executable for the supported default Local Environment path. `start`, `restart`, and `update` are phase-one planning entries: they return structured availability, reason codes, and next actions rather than pretending the CLI can start or update Desktop runtime sessions. When the target catalog contains SSH, container, provider, Gateway, or external Local UI entries, `redeven env` may resolve the target but still returns the lifecycle request as `supported=false` with `reason_code=unsupported_target_kind` unless the target is the default Local Environment.

Ask Flower environment context supplies routing data through the standard context action. When the accepted turn starts, Redeven can project these fields into Floret current-turn supplemental context metadata, but they remain product context and UI/audit facts rather than permission, remote execution proof, builtin target routing, or Redeven-owned provider history. The `redeven-environment` skill tells Flower to choose targets in this order: `execution_context.current_target_id`, then `target.target_id`, then `execution_context.source_env_public_id`, then `current`. The literal target `current` is accepted by Redeven target resolution as the current default environment alias.

For arbitrary target OS diagnostics, Flower should use `redeven targets exec --target <target> --command <agent-selected command> --json`. This command lets the agent choose OS-appropriate probes such as `date`, `uname`, uptime, disk, process, package manager, or service checks while Redeven owns target resolution, execution location, timeout, stdout/stderr, and exit-code provenance. The command supports the default Local Environment shell and saved local/SSH host runtime targets with key or agent SSH auth. Password SSH targets, container placements, provider environments, Gateway environments, and external Local UI targets return structured unsupported or unavailable results instead of falling back to ad hoc low-level commands.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
`redeven env` and `redeven targets exec` output is sanitized for Flower and automation. Target and runtime summaries omit runtime-control tokens and avoid leaking local state, config, socket, and agent home paths where those paths are not needed for the contract. Diagnostic output may include local state, lock, and socket paths when those paths are necessary to explain runtime attach state. Operation commands in the environment JSON contract may name Redeven product commands only. Target execution JSON must include `target_id` and `execution_location` when execution occurs. If an operation or target execution returns a structured unavailable, unsupported, or blocked result, Flower should explain that result and the product next action instead of inventing a lower-level workaround.

`terminal.exec` remains the local AI runtime shell. It starts an interactive PTY process with a local `process_id`, can be inspected with `terminal.read`, driven with `terminal.write`, and stopped with `terminal.terminate`. It can be used to invoke Redeven product commands, but it must not be described as remote execution unless the product command result itself contains target execution provenance such as `execution_location=ssh_target`.

concepts.title: Flower subagent runtime
concepts.description: Subagent lifecycle, delegated permission, summaries, membership, and human-detail contracts.

frontmatter.description: Subagent lifecycle, delegated permission, summaries, membership, and human-detail contracts.
frontmatter.title: Flower subagent runtime

concepts.summary: Flower subagents run through published Floret Host lifecycle APIs rather than a Redeven task manager. Redeven records product permission and approval context, exposes bounded model-facing summaries, and maps canonical child membership and detail for human inspection without creating child transcript ownership. Child tools refresh permission-derived surfaces and delegated approvals remain tied to explicit parent and child execution identity.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Flower subagents use the published Floret `Host` lifecycle rather than a Redeven-owned goroutine task manager. Main Flower runs, subagents, and compaction share the Floret-managed `stateDir/ai/floret_threads.sqlite` store, and Redeven accesses that store only through Floret public runtime APIs. Redeven caches one Floret subagent runtime per parent Flower thread so child controllers and the Floret host can outlive an individual parent turn, while restart recovery uses provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost` over the same published store contract without querying SQLite directly. Parent host initialization uses Floret `EnsureThread` instead of `ReadThread`. Parent Stop, CancelThread, and CancelRun are execution-lifecycle operations: after canceling the parent run they call Floret `CloseSubAgents` for the parent thread, which closes unfinished descendants, clears queued child input, and preserves completed history. A top-level parent hosted turn that exits failed, canceled, timed out, or with a Floret run error is also an execution-lifecycle terminal boundary: before Redeven performs product terminal cleanup, it classifies the parent reason (`parent_failed`, `parent_cancelled`, or `parent_timed_out`), calls Floret public `CloseSubAgents` through the active child host, parent host, or provider-free maintenance host, and republishes the resulting canonical child snapshots through `thread.subagents`. DeleteThread is a separate data-lifecycle operation documented in [Flower thread deletion coordination](flower-thread-deletion-coordination.md): Redeven atomically retires product data and persists an immutable cleanup snapshot first, then replays file cleanup, one Floret `ThreadMaintenanceHost.DeleteThread` tree deletion, and user read-state cleanup. Delete never calls `CloseSubAgents` as a precondition and never restores old rows as compensation. `Service.Close` finalizes pending terminal processes before releasing their top-level or child Hosts, then releases remaining local host resources. The `subagents` builtin validates `spawn`, `send_input`, `wait`, `list`, `inspect`, `close`, and `close_all`, then forwards lifecycle operations to Floret `SpawnSubAgent`, `SendSubAgentInput`, `WaitSubAgents`, `ListSubAgents`, `CloseSubAgent`, and `CloseSubAgents`; parent child-list refresh calls Floret `ListSubAgents` through the active host or `ThreadMaintenanceHost.ListSubAgents` and publishes `thread.subagents` patches instead of creating independent main-thread activity blocks. `wait` defaults to five minutes and caps requested waits at twenty minutes, while child runs pass a twenty-minute Floret subagent run timeout. The child `ThreadID` is the canonical subagent identity and is also returned as `subagent_id` for model-facing compatibility. Redeven stores a distinct product `child_run_id` for child permission snapshots and delegated approval refs; Floret execution `RunID` remains a per-engine-execution identity and is not interpreted as the product child run id. Delegated approval refs reject missing child run ids, child-thread aliases, and parent-run aliases. `spawn` persists a provisional child permission snapshot keyed by the real Floret `subagents` tool call id, calls `SpawnSubAgent`, and finalizes the snapshot only after host spawn succeeds; those stored snapshots remain product audit and permission records rather than child transcript ownership. Active child hosts install their own dynamic tool surface provider, derive the child surface from the latest parent thread permission on the next provider step and next tool dispatch, preserve child thread id and product `child_run_id` in host context, and emit `subagent.tool_surface.updated` when the child epoch changes. Task names and paths are labels, not lifecycle targets, and the model cannot supply a child `thread_id` or raw `fork_mode`. The parent model chooses `context_mode` on `spawn`: `mission_only` maps to `SubAgentForkNone` and `full_history` maps to `SubAgentForkFullPath`; after spawn, wait/list/detail payloads and parent activity presentation derive `context_mode` only from Floret `ForkMode` instead of a Redeven memory map or duplicate persistence. The thread-scoped child host registers the parent thread's delegated tool surface for the current permission snapshot, removes parent-only controls, and disables hidden Floret control surfaces. If the parent run's model/tool/session configuration changes, the runtime rebuilds the child host only when no child is active; active children continue on the existing host generation while still refreshing permission-derived tool surfaces. Child tools use the Floret invocation `ThreadID` and `TurnID` to verify execution scope, while Redeven product audit and delegated approval records use the explicit product `child_run_id` from host context rather than deriving it from Floret execution `RunID`. Child threads run with a Flower child prompt that forbids direct user input, states the visible tool list and context mode, and requires a complete but focused final handoff report.

Delegated approval lifecycle is not threadstore state. Redeven maps the current Floret pending approval snapshot into a thread-scoped in-memory Flower queue, validates parent ownership and opaque delegated references, and records only the user's authorization decision in product security audit. Resolved, expired, canceled, or restarted approval presentation is rebuilt from Floret rather than replayed from a Redeven request, event, idempotency, or outbox table. The parent Flower waiting projection remains the only actionable approval surface; subagent detail is a read-only mirror or locator.

Subagent data is intentionally split into model-facing summaries, parent timeline presentation, thread-level child state, and human detail. `subagents(action="spawn")` accepts `task_name`, the legacy `title` alias, a user-facing one-sentence `task_description`, an `agent_type`, and the full delegated `message`. Main-run prompt and tool descriptions ask the model for a 1-5 word English human-facing name such as `Safety Review`, `API Contract Review`, or `AI Research`. Before calling Floret, Redeven tokenizes snake case, kebab case, punctuation, and camel case, preserves common technical initialisms, applies Title Case, caps the result at five words and 48 ASCII characters, and falls back to a role-specific English task name when no usable token exists. The same canonical value is passed to Floret, persisted in lifecycle state, and returned through tool results and Flower summaries; historical stored names are not migrated. The task description remains the stable responsibility text shown in parent Flower UI, while live progress belongs in the child detail overlay. Model-facing `wait` results return bounded status `items` with child identity, task name, task description, agent type, status, timing, timeout facts, and `detail_ref`; they do not include child transcripts, tool calls, raw output, commands, stdout, stderr, `last_message`, `waiting_prompt`, or `result_digest`. Completed waits can include `final_handoff_report`, which carries each child’s final handoff text under a token budget without process detail. Timed-out waits can include `progress_summary`, which carries current status, waiting blockers when relevant, and suggested parent actions instead of execution logs. List and inspect remain bounded monitoring views with detail refs. Parent Flower timeline rows come from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection` activity segments and are projected to a public subagent payload before Flower renders them. That payload keeps only route/display facts such as `thread_id`, `subagent_id`, `parent_thread_id`, `action`, `status`, `task_name`, `title`, `task_description`, `agent_type`, canonical `items`, item status/timing, `timed_out`, `truncated`, counts, and public `error` or summary fields; it filters raw child content, private paths, detail refs, context/debug diagnostics, `last_message`, `waiting_prompt`, queued input counts, `can_*` control flags, and fields outside the current subagent payload allowlist. Flower activity rows render action-specific subagent titles, task/name previews, task descriptions, running elapsed time when available, exceptional status only when the child failed, canceled, timed out, or needs input, and an `Open messages` action derived only from the public `thread_id`/`subagent_id` payload fields.

The parent thread header Sub Agents dropdown is not a timeline reconstruction. Redeven lists child membership through Floret `ListSubAgents` on the active host, or through provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost.ListSubAgents` after runtime restart, maps that product-neutral state into `thread.subagents`, includes it in live bootstrap, and publishes the same list in `thread.patched` when subagent lifecycle events arrive. Flower builds the header badge and dropdown only from `thread.subagents`; it does not scan transcript blocks, `ActivityTimeline` rows, audit records, hidden sidecars, or thread list summaries. The shared UI projection derives a compatibility display name for historical snake/kebab/camel task names without mutating persisted state, then sorts children into stable active and ended priorities. The dropdown renders always-visible active and ended groups, exposes localized counts and statuses, focuses the first active row, supports arrow/Home/End navigation and Enter activation, and returns focus to the header trigger on Escape. If a thread list refresh summary omits `subagents`, that omission preserves selected-thread detail state; only a bootstrap or live patch that explicitly owns `subagents` can replace or clear the list. Redeven does not query Floret SQLite tables, write duplicate child membership into Redeven DB tables, or create child Flower metadata as a display source. The overlay reads `GET /api/ai/threads/{parent}/subagents/{child}/detail`, which validates the parent thread in Redeven, uses the active Floret host when present or a provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost` otherwise, calls Floret `ReadSubAgentDetail` through a parent-scoped public contract, maps Floret child not-found or ownership mismatch through `errors.Is` against Floret public runtime not-found sentinels to 404, and renders Floret's top-level canonical child activity timeline together with user/assistant messages, tool results, approvals, turn markers, compactions, errors, and custom rows from the child execution timeline. Preview-mode subagent detail is bounded inspection data and must not be used as the complete human transcript for child user or assistant messages; a complete human detail surface requires an explicit Floret display-detail contract while model-facing `wait`, `list`, and `inspect` results remain bounded. Event-row activity snapshots are not a display source, so later tool results or pending-tool settlements replace earlier running updates in the same canonical activity block. Child detail does not require a child row or child Flower metadata in Redeven threadstore, and subagents do not appear as child threads in the sidebar. Subagent model results use canonical `items` only; unsupported detail, transcript, raw-output, or non-allowlisted collection fields are filtered before they reach model-facing results or Flower activity payloads.

concepts.title: AI terminal tool runtime
concepts.description: PTY process handles, incremental output, termination, and Floret settlement ownership.

frontmatter.description: PTY process handles, incremental output, termination, and Floret settlement ownership.
frontmatter.title: AI terminal tool runtime

concepts.summary: Redeven owns PTY-backed terminal processes and bounded output retention, while Floret remains the canonical tool-lifecycle authority. The terminal tools expose one process handle across exec, read, write, and terminate; reads are incremental and termination waits for process-tree exit, output drain, and one owner-bound settlement. UI polling may observe process completion but cannot synthesize or retry canonical Activity settlement.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Terminal shell execution is a Redeven-owned process resource rather than a Floret-owned shell lifecycle. `terminal.exec` starts a PTY-backed process in the local AI runtime, immediately publishes a Floret `Invocation.UpdateActivity` presentation update with the public `process_id` read handle, waits only for the `yield_ms` initial wait, and returns either a completed result or a Floret pending tool result whose provider-visible handle is the same bare `process_id` used by `terminal.read`, `terminal.write`, and `terminal.terminate`. `yield_ms` is the only initial-wait argument and is not a hard timeout or automatic process termination request. Long-running commands continue outside the provider step. Redeven stores output as monotonic chunks of at most 4,000 bytes and retains at most 1,000,000 bytes by evicting complete oldest chunks. `Snapshot` returns the retained cumulative view for `terminal.exec`, final settlement, write/terminate responses, and cumulative UI replacement. `ReadAfter` returns only chunks after the caller's last consumed sequence; `terminal.read` uses a fixed five-second wait and 8,000-byte page, while the Flower process-read API uses a fixed one-second wait and 256,000-byte page. A valid Flower process read returns the current snapshot even when the process has already reached `success`, `error`, or `canceled`, so the terminal status can stop UI polling without becoming a canonical Activity lifecycle fact. A future cursor is invalid, an expired cursor returns the currently retained delta with `truncated=true`, and an empty delta preserves `last_seq=after_seq` with `first_seq=0`. Incremental results contain the single text field `output` plus `first_seq`, `last_seq`, `latest_seq`, `has_more`, process status, command, byte count, timing, and completion facts. They do not expose `stdout`, `stderr`, `latest_output`, caller-selected wait/size arguments, or field aliases.

Every `terminal.read` call requires exactly `process_id`, `description`, and `after_seq`. The first read uses zero; every later read copies the preceding result's `last_seq` unchanged, and `has_more=true` means another page is already available. The tool contract explicitly tells the model that `output` is new output since `after_seq`, never a replayed full snapshot, and that an empty value means no new output arrived during the wait. The bounded model-authored `description` uses the current user language, names the command or task being checked, and naturally distinguishes later checks. Redeven projects that description as the call activity label, emits no result label, and lets Floret preserve the call label while the result contributes the real command and that call's delta payload. The description is not duplicated into activity description or payload. `terminal.read` declares Floret's generic polling repeat policy, excludes only `description` from repeat identity through `AnnotationRepeatIdentityIgnoredArguments`, and returns a progress metadata token derived from `process_id`, `last_seq`, `status`, and `ended_at_ms`; changing presentation text cannot bypass no-progress detection. Redeven passes each incremental result once through `tools.Result.Text` and its independent display activity through `tools.Result.Activity`; it does not query Floret storage or persist a second mapped terminal state.

Every `terminal.terminate` call requires exactly `process_id` and a 1-120 rune `description` in the current user language that clearly names the command or task being stopped. Redeven uses `description` as the call activity label, keeps `process_id` in the compact call payload and terminal detail identity, and emits no result label, so Floret preserves the descriptive title instead of replacing it with the tool name. Termination has one ordered execution path: stop the Redeven PTY process, wait for process-tree reap and output drain, settle the original pending `terminal.exec` once through the process-bound Floret Host, consume the returned canonical Floret projection, remove the matching in-memory live draft when that projection is terminal, and return so AppServer can write its product operation audit. Published Floret v0.11.3 requires the complete `PendingToolSettlementTarget`, returns the same target with the settlement result, and validates the canonical result through `Validate()`; the creating Host can append settlement through its already-owned active thread without reacquiring the turn lease or starting another provider request. Redeven does not retry termination, issue `pkill` or `kill` compensation, swallow settlement errors, query Floret storage, or synthesize Flower state from audit rows.

Terminal snapshots and incremental reads carry `execution_location=local_runtime`, while the public Flower activity payload excludes host-local path/input fields such as `cwd`, `workdir`, or `stdin` as well as Floret pending lifecycle fields. Redeven caps active processes, retained output, stdin/input size, initial waits, and maximum runtime. Before a pending terminal process starts, Redeven requires the exact Floret `PendingToolSettlementTarget`, the creating top-level or subagent Host, and a one-shot finalizer. Missing ownership rejects process creation; Redeven never guesses the Host from a run registry and never opens a maintenance Host for terminal settlement. The finalizer becomes eligible only after the pending handle has been exposed to Floret and the PTY has completed reap, then invokes settlement once. `terminal.read` and `terminal.write` are pure PTY operations and cannot trigger, wait for, or retry settlement. `terminal.terminate` waits for reap, output drain, and that one settlement before returning. `Service.Close` terminates and finalizes all pending processes while their bound Hosts and the Floret store are still valid, then releases run and subagent resources. A ready settlement projection advances the shared durable `ThroughOrdinal` cursor and publishes canonical Flower state. An unavailable projection remains committed Floret state, emits diagnostic logging, and is not read back, retried, or locally synthesized. A settlement mutation failure is reported once by the finalizer and is not retried from read, write, cleanup, or later process access. Completion does not synthesize a duplicate provider-visible tool result, query Floret SQLite, persist a tool lifecycle copy, or automatically launch a follow-up model turn.

`terminal.exec` is the local AI runtime shell. Its schema describes local PTY process execution, with `yield_ms` as the only initial-wait argument. Terminal results include `execution_location=local_runtime` plus the resolved local working directory and process identity. Thread target context, Welcome environment context, or a target-shaped id does not change where this builtin runs. Remote or target diagnostics must use Redeven product commands whose own result payload carries target provenance.

concepts.title: AI tool approval runtime
concepts.description: Pending approval projection, queue ordering, conflict validation, and decision reconciliation.

frontmatter.description: Pending approval projection, queue ordering, conflict validation, and decision reconciliation.
frontmatter.title: AI tool approval runtime

concepts.summary: Floret owns pending tool-approval lifecycle, while Redeven maps the product-neutral snapshot into one thread-scoped Flower queue and applies product labels, policy, and delegated identity. Only the queue head is actionable; decisions validate queue generation, revision, event sequence, ownership, and current Floret pending state. Resolution returns a live cursor, reconciles against authoritative events, and treats stale or promoted actions as conflicts rather than replaying obsolete user intent.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Floret v0.11.4 validates pending approval collections and individual items before Redeven maps them, so missing identity, lifecycle, batch, timestamp, or argument-hash fields are contract errors rather than values Redeven may synthesize.

Current ordinary tool approvals come from Floret's product-neutral pending approval read model. Redeven calls `ListPendingApprovals` on the active Floret host, maps each generic pending approval to a Flower `FlowerApprovalAction` using `ToolPresentationSpec`, generic effects/resources/labels/host context, permission policy, delegated approval metadata, and Floret's zero-based `batch_index` plus `batch_size`, and then publishes that product projection through Flower live state. A thread-owned queue combines main tools, delegated child tools, and control confirmations. New actions receive immutable monotonic queue order; new main actions observed in one pending snapshot are first ordered by run, step, batch index, and action id, while later asynchronous or delegated actions append in registration order. Only the queue head is `primary_action` and user-decidable; every sibling remains a read-only locator. Approving the head releases that one waiter immediately and promotes the next action while the approved handler may still be running. Rejecting or timing out one action resolves only that action. The decision timeout begins on promotion, not while queued. Stop or cancellation closes every unresolved waiter, records canceled state, and clears the queue. The thread summary status is an effective product projection: `waiting_user` has first priority, an unresolved approval queue projects `waiting_approval`, and only then do active run or persisted terminal states apply. Lightweight thread list summaries do not carry approval actions or queue details, and their omission is never an authoritative clear of selected-thread live approval state. Pending approval summaries derive their user-visible labels from the same Redeven tool presentation projection used for Floret invocation activity; Floret carries that sanitized activity presentation through approval lifecycle observations, so a requested approval is rendered as the same tool activity item with `requires_approval`, `approval_state=requested`, and `status=waiting`, not as a second approval row or a UI-layer pending approval backfill. `requires_approval` records that the invocation participated in the approval lifecycle; once Floret resolves the approval, approved pending/running/success tool rows keep that history without becoming a locked current approval row in Flower. Activity timeline rows remain audit and locator material and expose no competing Allow or Deny buttons. Submitting an ordinary approval validates queue generation/revision, head identity, expected event sequence, action revision/version, surface epoch, user/thread/run ownership, and the current Floret pending snapshot before calling Redeven's approver channel. Every successful ordinary, control-confirmation, or delegated decision returns the cursor of its persisted `approval.resolved` live event through the existing `current_cursor` response field. The shared Flower surface enters loading synchronously before transport, then uses that receipt cursor plus the current stream generation to wait for an authoritative promoted action, explicit empty queue, or terminal thread. A newer generation is treated as resync and judged from its snapshot rather than compared to the old cursor. If live projection does not reconcile within 1500 ms, the surface performs one canonical fallback reload; missing approval fields preserve the frozen card, while explicit empty approval actions, a null/zero queue, or terminal state settle it. Any stale, duplicate, already-resolved, timed-out, unavailable, or non-head decision is returned as HTTP 409 with the stable `AI_APPROVAL_CONFLICT` error code. The shared Flower surface reloads canonical state on that conflict and retries at most once only when the same action id is still current and actionable with refreshed CAS fields; if another action has been promoted or the old action has resolved, the stale decision is discarded without user-facing send-failure feedback. The task-complete confirmation surface is a Redeven `control_confirm` product confirmation for a Floret control signal, but it shares the same product queue arbitration.

concepts.title: AI tool permissions and dispatch
concepts.description: Tool registration, scheduling, permission, readonly, and target-routing contracts.

frontmatter.description: Tool registration, scheduling, permission, readonly, and target-routing contracts.
frontmatter.title: AI tool permissions and dispatch

concepts.summary: Redeven owns the product tool registry, presentation metadata, concrete handlers, permission policy, and approval UX, while Floret owns the generic invocation lifecycle. Visible tools are rebuilt from the current thread permission before provider steps and local dispatch, so stale model calls cannot bypass the current product policy. Ordinary calls in one model response may execute concurrently, while dependent work must wait for a later response.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The builtin registry includes readonly file/search/fetch helpers, standard file mutation, patch, terminal, web search, OKF index/search/open, todo, interaction, skill, and subagent tools. Flower's product permission is `permission_type`, with `readonly`, `approval_required`, and `full_access` as the only current permission values. `read_file`, `read_files`, `rgrep`, `find`, and `web_fetch` are readonly-exclusive tools for the `readonly` surface; standard shell and mutation tools are not visible there. `approval_required` and `full_access` expose the standard host tool surface instead of those readonly-exclusive helpers. `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open` are read-only Redeven repository knowledge tools for directory discovery, short concept search, and detailed concept opening; web search is non-mutating product behavior but an open-world network effect in Floret's engine contract. `ToolPresentationSpec` is the single product display source for renderer, grouping, operation, label fields, fallback labels, compact call payload fields, result payload fields, and activity chip fields. Redeven projects builtins into Floret tool definitions with effects, read-only/destructive/open-world flags, resource extractors, static permission defaults, dynamic `PermissionFor`, and activity projection. Tool execution normalizes success summaries, errors, truncation, provenance, and builtin dispatch results so the chat activity timeline can render compact tool activity instead of raw low-level payloads; generic success summaries and event-code details remain model/audit material and are not copied into Flower expanded detail bodies by default. Flower activity detail rendering is Redeven product presentation over the Floret timeline: failures expand to a semantic `error` block that shows only the public failure reason, terminal rows expand to a read-only terminal output panel whose command detail includes the public `process_id` when present, file and patch rows render contents or diffs, web/search rows render result cards, question rows render prompts and choices, completion rows render outcome sections, todo rows render todo lists, subagent rows render delegated task summaries with message-entry actions, and generic structured rows show only allowlisted product detail fields. Flower does not fall back to raw `status`/`kind`/`tool`/`item` field tables when a renderer has no useful detail payload, and it filters non-actionable text such as `tool execution completed`, event codes, `success`, `completed`, or `ok`.

Ordinary tool scheduling follows the model's response boundaries. When one model response contains multiple ordinary tool calls, published Floret v0.8.0 starts the whole batch concurrently; each call independently validates arguments, extracts resources, checks permission, waits for approval when required, dispatches, projects output, and records audit facts. Result arrays and provider transcript entries retain the model's original call order, while dispatch and result observations retain actual start and completion order. A failed, rejected, or timed-out call does not stop an independent sibling. A dependent call must be emitted by the model in a later response after the prerequisite result is visible. Neither Floret nor Redeven infers dependencies or conflicts from tool names, arguments, effects, resources, filesystem paths, shell commands, permissions, or approval requirements, and the tool registry contains no scheduling-safety metadata. Main and subagent prompts state this response-boundary contract. `MaxToolCalls=200`, context cancellation, per-call timeouts, permission and approval gates, output limits, terminal active-process capacity, and other resource limits remain protections; they are not ordering policies. Ordinary calls in a mixed response close before Floret reevaluates control signals such as `ask_user` or `task_complete`.

Floret owns the permission lifecycle. Redeven decides product policy from the current thread `permission_type` and tool metadata, then returns allow/ask/deny through the Floret `Approver` before tool dispatch. Each hosted turn installs a dynamic Floret tool surface provider: before each provider step it rebuilds the visible tools, hosted control signals, system prompt, host context, capability contract, and `PermissionSnapshot`; before local dispatch it refreshes the snapshot again so a stale model tool call cannot bypass the current thread policy. The snapshot supplies the prompt capability list, visible tool names, Floret registry names, per-tool approval decision, registry/schema/presentation hashes, and an epoch used in `tool_surface.updated` run events. In `approval_required`, shell and mutating host actions ask before running; in `full_access`, per-tool approval is skipped while schema validation, resource extraction, target routing for eligible tools, output limits, product operation audit, and Floret activity projection still apply. Terminal process creation, input, output access, and termination additionally require effective write and execute permission, so an execute+read runtime policy cannot become a general-purpose shell through Flower. Command classification remains useful for audit and presentation, but it is not the source of permission decisions. Already-sent provider requests cannot be rewritten retroactively, but saved thread permission changes affect the next Floret provider request and the next local tool dispatch. `subagents` is a delegation-control tool visible in all three permission types, and its own `spawn`, `send_input`, `wait`, `list`, `inspect`, `close`, and `close_all` invocations do not require approval. That allow decision covers only the delegation control action itself; child runs receive a derived dynamic tool surface from the latest parent thread permission, and child tool invocations still flow through Floret permission/resource/approval lifecycle. A child approval-required action can delegate the ask back through the parent approver path; direct child user input remains disabled. Tool handlers execute already-approved Redeven domain actions, while Floret remains the single persistent source of truth for tool identity, arguments, lifecycle state, results, errors, completion output, and Activity projection. Redeven product audit records only user operations, policy, routing, and permission facts and never becomes a queryable tool-state copy or Flower display source.

Readonly-exclusive helpers are not general convenience aliases. `read_file`, `read_files`, `rgrep`, `find`, and `web_fetch` are visible only in `readonly`; `approval_required` and `full_access` use the standard host surface instead. `web_fetch` is a host-provided Redeven tool with SSRF protections, manual redirect validation, resolver and dial-time public IP checks, proxy suppression, body and MIME limits, and public-network-only semantics. DNS-derived VPN fake-IP answers in `198.18.0.0/15` are allowed for named hosts so common transparent-proxy/VPN setups can fetch public pages, but literal `198.18.0.0/15` URL hosts and other blocked local/private/reserved targets remain denied. It is not a Floret builtin and must not appear as a Floret core tool or a capability available to non-readonly permission types.

When a thread is configured for explicit target routing, the runtime forwards target-scoped builtin calls through `TargetToolExecutor`. The target executor receives a `TargetToolCall` containing `target_id`, `tool_name`, sanitized arguments, and required capabilities. The run layer returns a result payload that preserves or injects `target_id` and `execution_location`, so target-routed tool results cannot lose provenance before they reach the model or activity timeline.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Tool names are not aliases for deleted knowledge-era tools. Current repository knowledge access uses `okf.index`, `okf.search`, and `okf.open`: index for broad directory discovery, search for short candidate lists, and open for detailed concept content. OKF is an embedded project corpus and does not access the internet; external, current, recent, news, third-party, market, pricing, and general web facts must use direct authoritative URLs or web search discovery instead.

Target provenance is part of the tool contract, not a UI hint. Flower must not infer remote execution from thread context alone; it can only claim remote or target execution when a tool result or Redeven product command returns explicit execution provenance.

concepts.description: Redeven-owned Docker and Podman business capability contract for ReDevPlugin adapter registration.

frontmatter.description: Redeven-owned Docker and Podman business capability contract for ReDevPlugin adapter registration.
frontmatter.title: Container resources capability

concepts.summary:
Redeven owns the container resources capability as business-resource adapter
surface, not as plugin-platform core. The active v1 contract is
`redeven.capability.container_resources.v1` with capability id
`redeven.capability.container_resources`; it lives under `spec/capabilities/`
and `internal/capabilities/containers` rather than under `internal/plugins*` or
ReDevPlugin-owned `spec/plugin` paths.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The contract covers Docker and Podman engines, container status/list/inspect,
start preflight/start, stop/restart/remove, logs tail, and image pull method
names. Docker and Podman are distinct engine identities, not display labels.
Every container-targeting request carries `engine` and `container_id`, and the
target identity is the tuple `(engine, container_id)`. UI rows, plugin
manifest schemas, dangerous confirmation hash fields, and adapter dispatch must
not collapse identity to a bare container id or container name because Docker
and Podman can expose overlapping ids or names on the same host. The JSON
schema is closed-world and defines request/response DTOs, minimal container
summaries, inspect summaries, action results, bounded log batches, image pull
results, runtime summaries, target summaries, risk flags, and the start
preflight plan. The Go DTO constants and schema enum are bound by tests so
method names, schema identity, object closure, and engine fields cannot drift
silently.

Redeven also carries an integration-only generated plugin fixture for the
official Containers experience under
`internal/capabilities/containers/testdata/generated_plugins/containers_integration/`.
That fixture models the expected ReDevPlugin manifest surface over this
business capability: one `container_runtime` capability binding, the exact
`Methods()` method set, read/write/execute/delete permission coverage,
risk-based confirmation for `containers.start`, required confirmation for
stop/restart/remove, subscription semantics for logs tail, and cancelable
operation semantics for mutable actions and image pull. The fixture is tested
against Redeven's Go constants and schema fields, but it is not a
ReDevPlugin platform parser, package schema, runtime bridge, or substitute for
consuming released ReDevPlugin artifacts.

`Adapter` is the Redeven-owned business adapter for this capability. It wraps
an `EngineClient`, resolves the first available local engine only for requests
that are explicitly engine-optional, maps status/list/inspect/action/log/image
results into the public DTOs, and feeds observed inspect metadata into
`BuildStartPreflightPlan` for `containers.start` confirmation. Container
actions, inspect, logs, and preflight requests bind the selected engine and
target container id together. `BuildStartPreflightPlan` validates the selected
engine and target container id, normalizes capability lists and mount/device
summaries, computes a stable `target_hash`, and returns image, runtime, risk
level, risk flags, `requires_admin`, and a short summary.

`Adapter.CallMethod` is the Redeven-side method dispatcher used by the
registered ReDevPlugin capability adapter. It accepts a declared `Method` plus
raw JSON, requires `schema_version` to match the active capability contract,
decodes requests with unknown fields rejected, routes to the existing typed
adapter methods, and returns the same typed DTOs. This keeps JSON boundary
validation and method-to-DTO mapping in the business capability package rather
than duplicating it in product route glue. It is not a manifest parser,
package validator, token issuer, or alternate ReDevPlugin method router.

`Adapter.CallOperationMethod` is the Redeven-side operation dispatcher used by
the registered ReDevPlugin capability adapter. It accepts a Host-owned
`operation_id`, a declared operation method, and raw JSON, then routes
`containers.start`, `containers.stop`, `containers.restart`,
`containers.remove`, and `images.pull` through the matching operation-scoped
typed adapter method. The adapter validates the normal method request, binds
the Host-owned id to a scoped context cancel function, rejects duplicate active
ids, and unregisters the id when the operation returns or the parent context is
canceled. `Adapter.CancelOperation` looks up the active id, optionally checks
the method, and calls the stored cancel function. This table is limited to
in-flight container adapter work and does not create a ReDevPlugin operation
store, route, ticket, audit path, or platform operation manager.

`CLIClient` is the concrete local engine client. It shells out to Docker or
Podman with a bounded timeout, probes `version --format "{{json .}}"`, lists
containers through `ps --no-trunc --format json`, inspects one container through
`inspect`, executes container actions through explicit argv, pulls images, and
reads bounded non-follow log batches through `logs --timestamps`. `FollowLogs`
is a separate streaming hook that shells out to `logs --follow --timestamps`,
parses lines incrementally, and appends them to a caller-provided `LogLineSink`.
The default channel sink is fail-closed: a full sink returns stable
backpressure instead of buffering unbounded log data. Image pull also runs
through the same timeout-scoped command boundary, so parent context cancellation
and command timeout cancellation are propagated to the Docker/Podman helper.
The real process runner returns the context error after `exec.CommandContext`
terminates a command, preserving stable `context.Canceled` /
`context.DeadlineExceeded` semantics for ReDevPlugin operation cancel dispatch.
The parser accepts Docker's newline-delimited JSON list
format and Podman's JSON array list format, prefers server/engine version fields
when probing status, converts Docker-like inspect payloads into the minimal
internal `EngineContainer` shape used by the adapter, parses pull digests when
the engine reports one, and parses timestamped log lines into stable millisecond
timestamps.

`TestCLIClientRealEngineSmoke` is an opt-in local integration smoke for real
Docker or Podman engines. It is skipped unless
`REDEVEN_CONTAINERS_ENGINE_SMOKE=1` is set, can be scoped with
`REDEVEN_CONTAINERS_ENGINE_SMOKE_ENGINES`, and defaults to a BusyBox image
unless `REDEVEN_CONTAINERS_ENGINE_SMOKE_IMAGE` overrides it. When enabled, it
pulls the image, creates a disposable container, verifies inspect/list/start,
observes a bounded log marker, verifies the real `FollowLogs` streaming path
can read the same marker and stop through context cancellation, restarts/stops
the container, and removes it with cleanup. This gives release and developer
machines a true engine smoke without making ordinary CI require a Docker or
Podman daemon.

`TestCLIClientRealEnginePullCancelSmoke` is a separate opt-in cancellation
smoke. It is skipped unless `REDEVEN_CONTAINERS_ENGINE_PULL_CANCEL_SMOKE=1` is
set, uses the same engine selector as the regular smoke, and defaults to a
non-routable registry reference unless
`REDEVEN_CONTAINERS_ENGINE_PULL_CANCEL_IMAGE` overrides it. When enabled, it
runs a real Docker or Podman `pull` through the CLI helper with a short timeout
and requires the command to stop with `context.DeadlineExceeded`. This proves
the local engine helper can cancel a live image pull process when the mounted
ReDevPlugin operation cancel adapter dispatches to the Redeven business
adapter.

The v1 preflight flags cover privileged containers, host network/PID/IPC
namespaces, host devices, added Linux capabilities, Docker or Podman socket
mounts, bind mounts, sensitive mount paths, secret-like environment variables,
secret-like labels, persistent restart policies, and image references that are
not digest-pinned. Critical and host-control risks require admin approval in
the plan, while ReDevPlugin confirmation and token enforcement remain released
platform contracts before the business adapter executes.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Container DTOs are intentionally minimal. They do not expose raw inspect JSON,
raw command stdout, raw environment variable values, raw label values, or
sensitive host mount paths. Environment and label data are reduced to counts;
sensitive mount and device paths are replaced with a redacted marker while still
preserving risk flags. `TailLogs` remains bounded to at most 1000 non-follow
lines in this adapter, so plain request/response calls cannot accidentally
become unbounded subscriptions. Streaming is exposed only through the explicit
`FollowLogs` hook and requires a sink that owns backpressure and cancellation.
The released ReDevPlugin stream bridge must still own product route tickets,
stream-close audit, and client read semantics before the official Containers
plugin exposes follow logs to users. This preserves enough evidence for a
professional confirmation UI without turning the preflight plan into a secret
transport.

This contract is a Redeven-owned capability schema, not a ReDevPlugin platform
schema. Redeven may evolve the concrete Docker/Podman adapter and local CLI
client here, but plugin identity, lifecycle, permission, confirmation, token,
lease/quota, audit, and revocation checks must still be performed by released
ReDevPlugin artifacts before any container adapter method is called. The
official Containers plugin must expose canonical `container_id` fields in list
and inspect responses and must pass `engine` back on every action. The local
operation id table is only a business-adapter cancel target for a Host-owned
id; official operation records, cancel-request persistence, mounted HTTP
routes, stream tickets, audit events, disable/uninstall policy, and final
operation lifecycle remain ReDevPlugin-owned. If integration needs a reusable
platform behavior not represented by a released ReDevPlugin contract, that
behavior is added upstream first instead of copied into Redeven.

concepts.summary:
Env App is built as a Redeven-specific shell on top of published floe-webapp UI/runtime primitives, floe-webapp protocol connectivity, Floeterm terminal-web components, and Flowersec controlplane artifact helpers.

Env App composes published floe-webapp, Floeterm, and Flowersec web packages.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The released browser dependency set includes Floeterm terminal-web v0.5.19 in addition to Floe Webapp v0.37.3 and Flowersec Core v0.25.0.

The UI package pins Floe Webapp v0.37.3 and Flowersec Core v0.25.0 from the public registry. `EnvAppShell` imports floe-webapp providers, layout primitives, icons, and protocol hooks; terminal surfaces use Floeterm `TerminalCore` and `PagedTerminalOutputCoordinator`; controlplane services call Flowersec controlplane helpers to exchange entry tickets for connect artifacts; and local package-lock files are the audited dependency source for the embedded UI build. Activity and Workbench terminals map Redeven attach/history RPCs and shell-activity projection into the same upstream output coordinator. `TerminalSessionRuntime` calls `beginAttach` before subscribing to live output, then completes that generation with the Runtime's atomic history boundary; output produced during the attach round trip remains retained, while a stale acknowledgement cannot complete a newer generation. The separate Runtime attach generation is owned by the stable protocol transport proxy, so Activity, Workbench, component remounts, RPC facade recreation, and reconnect share a monotonic sequence for one provider lifetime. A superseded current interactive surface retries with a newer generation under view, active-session, and focus ownership fences; a background surface waits until it regains those owners. Session disappearance, connection teardown, and transport disconnect end the obsolete surface without presenting a false terminal-recovery error. Initial replay blocks terminal input only until explicit coverage establishes a baseline and `TerminalCore.writeHistory` reports parser completion; background gap recovery and automatic retry after that fence remain non-blocking. The coordinator owns sparse sequence coverage, retained-live bounds, retry state, snapshot-generation validation, truncation rebases, cancellation, writer completion, and coverage commit ordering. `pause()` returns only after the current writer and its coverage commit are quiescent, and the cancelled recovery cannot start another transform/write batch afterward. Redeven owns only RPC conversion, source tagging, ordered parser transformation, terminal visibility, stable localized status presentation, diagnostics entry, and product activity callbacks. Shell, OSC, working-directory, and visible-output side effects are emitted only from the coordinator's accepted ordered transform path; the host does not keep a parallel payload projection cache. Normal catch-up stays silent, while delayed retry and degraded states use a reserved footer: 28 px on desktop and 44 px while the Floe mobile keyboard is visible. Initialization autofocus requires focus ownership to remain empty or inside the same current-generation terminal. Session-selection focus uses a separate generation-scoped intent captured at request time; it retries after the selected Runtime becomes interactive only while the committed target, view ownership, keyboard policy, and original sidebar/body/origin-or-target-terminal focus ownership remain valid. `TerminalSessionRuntime` owns the Core, coordinator, snapshot, and per-session focus lifecycle, while `TerminalSessionNavigator` renders the desktop sidebar and mobile drawer from lightweight session state without instantiating a Core. `TerminalPanel` remains the product orchestration and composition owner instead of introducing a second terminal state model. Redeven's adaptive working-set manager does not impose a Core count: it keeps all opened cores warm below a device-derived byte budget, protects the current and recently switched sessions, and asks the released Floeterm core to capture or restore memory-only snapshots only from idle work. A hibernated core releases renderer resources while its PTY, output coordinator, unread state, and session-list row continue independently. Flowersec reconnects keep one active promise per transport configuration, proxy redirects are returned to the browser, and Service Worker chunk credits prevent a fast runtime from outrunning a slow consumer while legacy workers continue without the optional capability. Local Direct reconnects select `AllowPlaintextForLoopback` for loopback locations or a `createNetworkPlaintextPolicy` bound to the exact browser IP after the Runtime has activated explicit network exposure; construction failure blocks the connection and never retries with an unrestricted policy.

Runtime reconnect configuration uses Flowersec's discriminated `ArtifactSource` contract directly. Both Local UI and remote controlplane paths use `refreshable` sources because each reconnect must mint a fresh credential; no fixed artifact is reused or given an auto-reconnect escape path. Entry-ticket exchange uses `createControlplaneArtifactSource` and `acquire`, forwarding abort and trace context. Env App constructs one stable reconnect config for each transport mode so hard reconnects preserve Flowersec trace and source-consumption state. Local Direct explicitly allows plaintext only for canonical loopback authorities and enables 15-second Yamux liveness with a 10-second timeout. Remote Tunnel explicitly requires TLS and retains Flowersec's tunnel liveness default. Both paths select 64 KiB outbound records and explicit WebSocket and Yamux queue, frame, stream, and receive-memory limits.

After an already opened Env App loses connectivity, Redeven projects published Flowersec `onDiagnosticEvent` reconnect events into one immutable product recovery snapshot. The `reconnect` stage, event code, result, and monotonic `attempt_seq` are the source for fast-reconnect attempts; Redeven does not recreate Flowersec's retry loop. Runtime availability probes return structured availability, access state, failure code, and HTTP status, and error classification uses only those fields and error types rather than message fragments. A Desktop-hosted session also subscribes to its sender-scoped transport-recovery snapshot. While that bridge is `waiting`, `connecting`, or terminally `failed`, Env App pauses its own Runtime probe loop; a recovered bridge advances directly to protocol reconnect. Generation and revision checks discard stale Desktop updates, and no layer replays the request that observed the disconnect.

Terminal diagnostics are renderer-local and content-free. Protocol capture projects `terminal.sessionAttach` to the numeric Runtime `attach_generation`, dimensions, acknowledgement, history boundary, and a numeric RPC failure code, `terminal.input` to byte count, and `terminal.history` to page, chunk, byte, coverage, generation, reset, and truncation metadata; command, output, session ID, connection ID, path, raw failure message, and raw failure cause are not retained. Raw fetch/RPC capture runs only while Debug Console is open. Sanitized recovery lifecycle events always enter a bounded 160-event ring so a later diagnostics action can inspect the failure; export includes the bounded client events and dropped count. The diagnostic stream keeps surface generation and Floeterm coordinator attach generation in recovery events, Runtime `attach_generation` in the session-attach protocol projection, and Runtime history generation in history and recovery metadata, using distinct field names rather than collapsing them into one generation. Recovery events use renderer-lifetime pseudonymous session references. The terminal diagnostics action opens the trace view using only the pseudonym, surface generation, and stable error code. Renderer `performance.mark` entries for attach start/ack, baseline queued/parser committed, interactive, and first live output share the renderer monotonic clock. Performance mark names use `redeven:terminal:<milestone>:<trace-id>`, so interleaved Activity and Workbench recovery traces cannot clear or overwrite one another.

Each `TerminalSessionRuntimeStatus` carries the diagnostics query derived from that runtime's own trace. Activity and Workbench surfaces for the same terminal session therefore keep distinct surface generations even when their failures interleave; the footer never resolves diagnostics through a session-global latest trace. The terminal runtime root reports `aria-busy=true` only until the initial baseline parser commit makes the surface interactive. Background retry or degraded recovery remains usable and does not set the surface busy again. The tab activity tracker promotes already-observed background provisional unread to confirmed unread before attach-generation changes, history rebases, or ordinary reattach; only an explicit user clear-history reset may discard that provisional state.

Env App keeps only bootstrap, access, protocol, and shell chrome in the initial module. Activity surfaces, Workbench, Terminal, Monitor, Files, Codespaces, Ports, Flower, Codex, Settings, Plugins, Debug Console, audit, file browser, and file preview hosts are loaded through feature boundaries when activated. Flower and Codex providers are scoped to the surfaces that need them, so a connected shell does not initialize AI settings, model catalogs, or thread lists by default. Feature CSS follows the same boundary: Iosevka loads with Terminal, shared Flower and chat styles load with Flower entry points, and Codex styles load with Codex entry points.

Activity navigation does not replace the Activity Shell or change its outer provider structure. `ActivityAppsMain` is the lifetime owner for Activity surfaces: a surface mounts on first activation and remains mounted but hidden while another Activity surface is active. This preserves file browser path, expanded directories, filtering, view mode, selection, scroll position, and equivalent local state in Terminal, Monitor, Flower, Codex, and other registered Activity surfaces. Activity and Workbench are themselves two lazily visited views in one `KeepAliveStack`; after first activation, switching display mode changes visibility and activation after paint without destroying either view. Activity navigation, display-mode navigation, and Workbench widget activation therefore share the same lifetime rule: lazy before first visit, stable DOM/provider/data-flow identity after first visit.

Connection recovery preserves those lifetime owners. Activity and Workbench each mount the same `ConnectionRecoveryView` once at their content root while their existing page DOM remains mounted. The underlying content container becomes `inert`, pointer-disabled, and `aria-hidden`; Terminal, Files, Monitor, Workbench widgets, and other surfaces do not own separate connection curtains or continue accepting stale input. The recovery presentation derives a fixed applicable-step timeline, exact completed-step percentage, real attempt counts, and only known retry timestamps from the immutable snapshot. Successful recovery remains visible for 1.5 seconds before the snapshot returns to idle and the original surface becomes interactive again. Terminal failure retains the window, focuses an alert heading, keeps technical details collapsed by default, and exposes only actions present in the Desktop snapshot. Raw HTTP status text is diagnostic detail rather than primary recovery copy.

The released floe-webapp selection controller separates immediate visual selection from committed content. Env App opts the Shell into UI-first Activity selection, uses after-paint activation for `ActivityAppsMain`, and uses another UI-first controller plus after-paint `KeepAliveStack` activation for Activity/Workbench mode changes. User input uses the delayed transaction path; initialization, restore, permission fallback, and other canonical corrections may commit synchronously. Persistence runs from the committed state rather than from a visual preview. Navigation does not suspend hidden surfaces, stop subscriptions, hibernate terminal cores, refetch warm content, or recreate providers; only visibility-dependent focus, resize, scroll restoration, viewport movement, and measurement may run after the target content has painted.

Codex keeps its heavy provider and feature CSS behind the lazy `CodexActivitySurface` boundary. The surface creates one shared Codex provider for the page and sidebar. The page stays inside the kept-alive Activity view, while the sidebar is projected through a Solid Portal into a lightweight Shell-owned host that exists only while Codex is active and permitted. The Portal mount remains stable when that host is removed or recreated, so returning to Codex moves the existing sidebar tree instead of recreating the provider, page, thread state, drafts, or sidebar-local state. If the Codex chunk resolves after navigation has already left Codex, it may finish mounting inside the hidden kept-alive surface without replacing the Shell or another Activity page.

The local bootstrap reuses the access status returned by `getLocalRuntime()` instead of issuing a second access-status request. Production builds enforce compressed initial-resource budgets of 600 KiB JavaScript, 120 KiB critical CSS, and 720 KiB total. The budget check also rejects initial HTML references to Markdown, KaTeX, Mermaid, Monaco, Excel, PDF, Shiki, Flower, or Codex feature assets.

Terminal attachment ownership and native autofocus are separate contracts. The Floe mobile keyboard may keep the current Activity or Workbench terminal attached and retry a superseded Runtime generation while deliberately leaving the system textarea unfocused. Every reload entry, asynchronous attach boundary, and queued retry checks the surface disposal fence, so an unmounted panel cannot perform a ghost attach after teardown.

Upstream TerminalCore and coordinator loggers are strict no-ops inside the product. Redeven emits only stable event and error codes with numeric or boolean metadata, never raw `Error` objects, upstream messages, terminal content, or encoded content. Console privacy tests reject both a unique raw secret and its base64 form independently.

Renderer validation keeps distinct evidence boundaries. The built-dist shell smoke serves only the production `dist/env` files and proves a nonblank root, expected title, absence of framework overlays or browser errors, content-hashed JavaScript/CSS, and a compilable content-hashed WASM renderer. The terminal recovery process carrier separately starts a temporary production Redeven Runtime and drives the real Local UI, Direct WebSocket/RPC, terminal-go, PTY, TerminalPanel, coordinator, parser, and canvas path in headless Chromium. It exercises Workbench and Activity panel surfaces across reused and fresh browser contexts, verifies parser-committed recovery for 64 KiB, 448 KiB, and the exact 8 MiB retained-byte boundary, compares the recovered canvas against seeded history evidence, and sends a filesystem-backed input probe through the terminal. Neither test starts Redeven Desktop, and neither is described as a packaged Electron test. Reports contain only fixture classes, requested, retained, and fetched byte counts, pseudonymous references, generation and coverage metadata, timings, stable failure stage codes, and runner fingerprint data. Standard GitHub-hosted jobs use the timings as raw evidence, not as percentile claims.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
This concept only holds while Env App continues to consume published upstream packages instead of local sibling checkouts or ad-hoc replacements for protocol, terminal, reconnect, and controlplane artifact handling. Redeven may select generic resource values and map product credential acquisition into `ArtifactSource`, but must not recreate Flowersec reconnect state, WebSocket backpressure, Yamux liveness, E2EE framing, RPC scheduling, or host-scoped plaintext policy semantics. Redeven must not recreate Floeterm output recovery, snapshot formats, resource estimation, or shell lifecycle generation in product code. The packaged Env App and Runtime must satisfy compatibility epoch 8 and the v0.10.0 minimum pair so the coordinator never treats missing coverage metadata or an atomic attach boundary as a successful baseline and the Env App never omits or misinterprets Local UI exposure state. Lazy boundaries change loading time, not product contracts: restored surface state, permission gates, Workbench activation, error recovery, and provider ownership must remain equivalent after a feature chunk resolves. Activity-specific feature providers must live inside their kept-alive surface boundary; an active-surface condition must not wrap or replace the Activity Shell. Activity/Workbench mode changes must not be treated as an unload boundary after either view has mounted. Sidebar portals may change their visible host, but the provider and kept-alive surface remain the state owners. The initial budget is a required build gate and must not be bypassed by renaming or preloading feature assets through a different entry.

The Env App recovery snapshot is a product presentation projection, not another protocol or Desktop transport mechanism. It must not infer retry timing, parse failure prose, invent attempts, add a backup URL, retry through public Local UI, migrate streams, rebind a stale session, or replay user requests. Explicit Start, Restart, Stop, and Update continue to hand off to Desktop Welcome and remain outside this post-open recovery flow.

concepts.summary: Redeven Local UI is the browser-facing endpoint runtime surface. It exposes the Env App proxy, Local UI status APIs, direct Flowersec session handoff, Browser Editor codespace routes, and port-forward routes from the same runtime-managed HTTP server.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

`localui.Server` is built with an agent, Code App app server, bind spec, runtime-control socket path, Local Environment identity, diagnostics store, and optional access gate. The handler mounts `/api/local/*`, `/_redeven_direct/ws`, `/_redeven_proxy/*`, `/cs/*`, and `/pf/*`. Password mode protects non-public routes, direct sessions are minted as short-lived Flowersec connect artifacts, and runtime responses include the normalized Runtime Service snapshot.

Local UI bind parsing accepts `localhost`, canonical loopback IP literals, concrete non-loopback unicast IPv4 and IPv6 literals, and the `0.0.0.0` and `::` wildcards. It never resolves DNS names. Network exposure requires a fixed port; dynamic port `0` remains available only for explicit loopback IP binds. A network bind is valid only when password authentication and the command-line plaintext exposure acknowledgement are both present. Wildcard startup enumerates active, non-loopback, same-family unicast interface addresses, excludes unspecified, multicast, link-local, zoned, and IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses, sorts them deterministically, and fails when no real access address remains.

Runtime starts two independent HTTP listener boundaries. The public Local UI listener records only exact canonical authorities for the actual bound or enumerated access IPs and ports, and every request passes that authority gate before route, cookie, WebSocket, or access-gate processing. A separate trusted listener binds `127.0.0.1:0`, mounts `HandlerForDesktopBridge()`, and is published as the required `RuntimeAttachEndpoint.local_ui_bridge_url`. It accepts canonical loopback authorities for native Desktop and placement-bridge traffic without weakening the public listener's exact-port authority rule. The private `0600` Desktop launch/status report carries this machine-only endpoint, but public startup events, health, access status, catalogs, display URLs, preferences, and renderer projections do not expose it. Runtime health returns the public `local_ui_url` and ordered `local_ui_urls` so a native Desktop health probe can travel through the trusted listener while refreshing user-facing addresses independently. Desktop validates the bridge value as an HTTP loopback-IP root with an explicit port and no credentials, query, or fragment. DNS names, fake localhost suffixes, alternate IPv4 notation, malformed ports, zones, mapped IPv6, non-loopback authorities, and unlisted public authorities are rejected.

The network server bounds request headers, request bodies, header-read time, read time, write time, idle time, and WebSocket frames. Responses receive CSP frame ancestry, content-type sniffing, referrer, permissions, and same-origin frame headers. Browser WebSocket upgrades require an exact same-scheme, same-authority Origin for the validated request authority. Runtime-control keeps its Desktop owner, bearer token, and loopback peer checks; its non-browser WebSocket may omit Origin, but any supplied Origin must still match its loopback authority.

Direct connect artifacts are one-time credentials, but resolution does not consume them. The Local UI resolver returns the E2EE PSK and an authenticated commit callback. Flowersec invokes that callback only after PSK authentication and before Yamux creation. The callback atomically removes the still-matching pending artifact, so a failed handshake does not exhaust a valid artifact and concurrent authenticated handshakes cannot both open sessions.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body: Local UI route behavior is part of the runtime trust boundary. Public Env App shell GET/HEAD requests may pass before local unlock so the shell can load, but local APIs, direct sessions, codespaces, and port-forward routes stay access-gated when password mode is enabled. Network exposure is plaintext HTTP: password authentication controls access but does not protect passwords, cookies, page resources, or non-Flowersec HTTP traffic from interception or modification. Flowersec protects its encrypted session payload only after the E2EE handshake completes. The trusted listener is reachable only through local loopback and `redeven desktop-bridge`; native Desktop, SSH, and container paths must not replace it with or fall back to the public listener. Runtime-control, Desktop model-source, and runtime management sockets remain loopback or local-socket protected regardless of Local UI exposure.
