concepts.title: Flower subagent detail presentation
concepts.description: Parent header membership, activity routing, and read-only child execution detail.

frontmatter.description: Parent header membership, activity routing, and read-only child execution detail.
frontmatter.title: Flower subagent detail presentation

concepts.summary: Flower derives parent header child membership from Floret ListSubAgents and opens child execution detail inside the parent surface without navigating to a child thread. Parent activity carries only sanitized route and display facts, while the read-only detail ledger uses parent-scoped Floret data and preserves live tailing, disclosure, and pagination state. Child detail is human inspection state and is not injected into parent model context.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Subagent presentation uses two separate public contracts. Thread-level child membership comes from Floret `ListSubAgents` through the active parent host or provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost`; Redeven maps that product-neutral snapshot into `thread.subagents` during live bootstrap and publishes the same list through `thread.patched` after runtime child events. Thread list summaries do not own `thread.subagents`: if a refresh summary omits the field, Flower preserves the selected thread detail value, and only an explicit detail or live patch value replaces it. The parent header Sub Agents dropdown is derived only from `thread.subagents`, so a runtime restart can restore the count and child list without scanning transcript activity blocks. New child names are canonical 1-5 word English presentation labels normalized before Floret spawn, while the UI humanizes historical snake/kebab/camel names and applies an English role fallback when no English words are available, without migrating stored state. Redeven does not read Floret store tables, create child threadstore rows, or persist a duplicate mapped child list. Parent timeline rows still come from Floret `ThreadTurnProjection` activity segments for the `subagents` tool. Their public payload is sanitized to route and display facts only: `thread_id`, `subagent_id`, `parent_thread_id`, task name/title, `task_description`, agent type, status, timestamps, counts, timeout/truncation flags, and public error/summary fields. It filters raw child content, host-local paths, detail refs, context/debug fields, `last_message`, `waiting_prompt`, `can_*` control flags, queued input counts, and fields outside the current subagent payload allowlist. Inline `Open messages` actions read only the public `thread_id`/`subagent_id` facts in the activity payload; there is no activity sidecar fallback. The dropdown is a header-triggered floating surface, not a page or side panel: it uses the shared Floe surface layer and named Flower layer tokens, displays neutral compact chrome with localized active/ended metrics, and keeps active and ended sections visible. Each row exposes one status icon, the canonical display name, one-line responsibility text, localized status, elapsed duration when timestamps are available, and a detail chevron. Active rows sort waiting-input, running, queued, then unknown; ended rows sort failed, timed-out, completed, then canceled, with update time as the secondary order. Opening the dropdown focuses the first active row; arrow keys, Home, End, Enter, and Escape provide complete keyboard navigation, and reduced-motion mode removes popover and status animation. Selecting a child never selects or navigates to the child thread; it loads parent-scoped detail through the surface adapter and opens a read-only Floe `FloatingWindow` over the current parent conversation. The detail window reuses the same timeline/message/activity renderers as the main thread, adds a bottom live status lane, tails active child detail incrementally, auto-follows only while the user is at the bottom, and exposes a scroll-to-latest affordance when the user reviews earlier output. Detail tailing continues while the child is queued, running, or waiting for input even when the current page reports `has_more=false`; terminal child status stops the tail, and transient tail errors stay in the detail dock with a longer retry interval. The detail tail and full child execution timeline are human UI state only; they are never injected into the parent model context by default. The overlay renders Floret `ReadSubAgentDetail` through the parent thread scope: user/assistant messages, tool results, approvals, turn markers, compactions, errors, and generic custom rows come from the child detail events, while tools render from the top-level canonical child activity timeline. Bounded preview detail rows are inspection data and must not be presented as complete child user or assistant output; a human detail surface that needs complete visible child output must consume an explicit Floret display-detail contract rather than mapping preview text into the transcript body. Tailing updates replace that stable activity block instead of appending event-row activity snapshots, so older running tool updates disappear when a later result or settlement becomes canonical. Detached reads use provider-free `ThreadMaintenanceHost` and do not require provider configuration, a child threadstore row, or child Flower metadata in Redeven. Summary rows, when present, are explicit Floret detail data rendered in the same read-only message model. Subagent model results and activity payloads support canonical `items` only, and unsupported detail, transcript, raw-output, or non-allowlisted collection fields are filtered before presentation.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Flower does not synthesize Sub Agents dropdown state from activity timeline sidecars, audit tables, provider-adapter side effects, child assistant text, Redeven child thread rows, or a separate transcript block type. `thread.subagents`, sourced from Floret `ListSubAgents`, is the only parent header child-list contract, and thread list summaries that omit `subagents` are not allowed to clear selected-thread detail state. Activity rows may expose `Open messages` only from sanitized public `thread_id`/`subagent_id` payload fields, not from hidden sidecars or fallback transcript scans. Full child execution detail belongs to the parent-scoped subagent detail API and is never injected into the parent model context by default. Subagent detail live tailing is a UI-only read path with request identity guards; it must not become a model-facing wait result, a second chat composer, or an alternate thread navigation target. Preview-only Floret detail rows cannot be used as a fallback for complete subagent output, and increasing a preview limit is not a substitute for a complete human-display contract. Redeven does not create read-only child thread rows for Floret subagents; child Flower rows are not a supported display source or compatibility path.

The Sub Agents detail window is a read-only execution ledger owned by the parent Flower surface, not a compact child chat. Its overview presents the canonical child status together with task description, agent type, and elapsed time. The ledger classifies projected user and system messages as collapsed delegated instructions or runtime constraints and keeps assistant narrative open for reading. Its presentation model groups adjacent pure canonical activity messages into one operation phase without rebuilding, copying, or persisting their activity items or statuses. Visible narrative, instruction, constraint, input, compaction, error, outcome, or mixed content/activity entries end the current phase, so tool work before and after an assistant explanation remains separate. A multi-operation phase renders one summary and preserves each canonical tool row underneath it; a phase containing one operation renders that tool row directly without a redundant one-operation heading. Successful phases larger than six operations start collapsed, while running, waiting, canceled, or failed phases remain open so active and exceptional work is not hidden. Each phase key derives from its first activity entry, preserving the user's disclosure state when live tailing appends or settles later tools. Terminal details omit an empty status dock; live model state, tail synchronization, tail errors, and pagination render the dock only while they have actionable content. Initial and incremental detail failures provide local retry actions without creating a child composer, changing parent-thread selection, or claiming that preview-mode rows are a complete transcript.

concepts.title: Flower terminal activity presentation
concepts.description: Canonical terminal activity, disclosure, animation, scrolling, and read-only controls.

frontmatter.description: Canonical terminal activity, disclosure, animation, scrolling, and read-only controls.
frontmatter.title: Flower terminal activity presentation

concepts.summary: Terminal activity is a renderer-first view over canonical Floret Activity items, not a second process lifecycle. Stable activity identity preserves disclosure and viewport state across live replacements; terminal output may poll Redeven process handles, but canonical status and completion remain projection-owned. Measured disclosure animation, local scrolling, and transcript following preserve user reading intent and reduced-motion behavior.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Terminal process activity remains part of that canonical projection. A running `terminal.exec` row receives its command, status, process id, cumulative retained snapshot, and sequence facts from the Floret `ActivityTimeline` payload produced by Redeven tool presentation metadata. `terminal.exec` publishes the process id as soon as the PTY starts through Floret's running activity update path, so `yield_ms` affects only when the agent receives the tool result, not when the user can inspect command progress. Each model-issued `terminal.read` activity uses its required user-language description as the compact row title, while the returned `payload.command` remains the real command shown only in expanded terminal details. Result presentation omits a label so Floret preserves the call intent, and Flower does not synthesize generic terminal-output titles. Each read remains a separate activity row and its expanded panel shows only that call's `output` delta: a first read can show phase 1 and the next read can show phase 2 without making the command appear to have executed again. `terminal.terminate` follows the same presentation contract: its required user-language description remains the row title through the successful result, the tool name is not used as a visible fallback, and the expanded command detail shows the real command plus the public `process_id` needed for inspection and diagnostics. That public payload must not expose host-local path or input fields such as `cwd`, `workdir`, or `stdin`.

Expanded terminal rows are read-only product presentation. Completed status, output, exit code, and duration come directly from the canonical Floret Activity payload and never call a Redeven completion-output endpoint. The shared terminal store applies two explicit sequence operations while the Activity remains running: a newer exec snapshot replaces the cumulative view, while a contiguous PTY process-read delta appends to it. Equal or older snapshots are ignored by sequence, an empty delta does not clear existing exec output, and an explicit `truncated=true` gap replaces the unavailable prefix with the retained delta. The store never decides cumulative-versus-incremental behavior with string prefix or substring checks. Flower and Env App send only the required `after_seq` process-read query for a running Activity with a public process id; server-owned waits and page limits are not UI options. Polling may continue while details are collapsed so a stored delta is not lost merely because it was not visible when it arrived. A valid process read can return a terminal `success`, `error`, or `canceled` snapshot when the PTY finishes between polls, including an empty delta whose `last_seq` remains equal to `after_seq`; that response stops local polling but can never change canonical Activity status, create lifecycle facts, or trigger settlement. Raw AppServer errors are logged for diagnostics and render only localized live-output or terminal-action failure text. The surface exposes icon-only actions to reveal and copy the full raw command and caps output at five visual line boxes using explicit font size, line height, padding, and border-box sizing; shorter output uses its natural height, while later lines increase only the local scroll height. Every pending terminal process binds its complete Floret settlement target and creating top-level or subagent Host before launch. After the pending handle is exposed and PTY reap completes, one finalizer performs one settlement through that owner; process reads, writes, cleanup, and later UI access never trigger or retry settlement, and no maintenance Host fallback exists. A ready settlement projection advances the same `ThroughOrdinal` cursor as every other projection and publishes the canonical `timeline.replaced` snapshot. An unavailable settlement projection remains committed Floret state, produces diagnostic logging, and is not read back or synthesized locally. `terminal.terminate` waits for process-tree exit, output drain, and finalization before returning, while `Service.Close` completes finalizers before releasing Floret Hosts and stores. Consequently, canonical terminal ordering and completion facts come only from durable Floret projections rather than a terminal-specific database, retry path, floor, refresh, or UI repair mechanism.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Flower activity details are renderer-first product views, not generic payload inspectors. `error`, `terminal`, `file`, `patch`, `web_search`, `question`, `completion`, `todos`, and `subagents` each have an explicit display model that prioritizes the information a normal user is trying to inspect: failure reason, terminal output, file content, diffs, search results, prompts and choices, outcomes, todo lists, or delegated task status. Failed tools render the public error message first and do not expose error codes, retryability flags, item ids, tool ids, or lifecycle diagnostics in the ordinary detail body. Successful tools do not show generic completion summaries or event-code details such as `tool execution completed`, `todos.updated`, `file.updated`, `success`, `completed`, or `ok`. A compact structured metadata block remains available only for allowlisted product details that do not belong to the primary renderer. Flower must not fall back to displaying every payload key, or raw `status`/`kind`/`tool`/`item` rows, as the main detail experience.

Flower activity disclosure is presentation state over the canonical activity lifecycle, not a second execution state machine. Every stateful activity view uses one semantic identity composed from thread, run, turn, and canonical `item_id`; status, payload, message block position, item position, and renderer object identity are not part of that identity. `timeline.replaced` may move an activity between message blocks, but it must reuse the same activity row, disclosure controller, detail component, and terminal viewport. A live projection may first expose the stable item without a renderer or detail payload and later enrich that same item; the row keeps its DOM identity, while the disclosure trigger remains bound and its native disabled state follows detail availability. Routine `pending` or `running` detail waits 400 ms before opening, so activity that settles sooner remains compact and never produces an expand-collapse flash. Opening uses a 360 ms measured-height animation. An automatically opened terminal command starts its 1200 ms completion hold only after the canonical `success` or `canceled` presentation and latest visible output have completed their terminal presentation frame; another settled output frame restarts the full hold. The detail then uses a 300 ms closing animation. Other routine activity remains intent-anchored. Error, waiting, approval, or blocking attention opens immediately and remains open until the user changes it. A manual expand or collapse overrides later lifecycle updates for that stable activity item, and pointer, focus, wheel, or touch interaction inside automatically opened detail converts it to an explicit open choice so the surface cannot close content while the user is reading, selecting, scrolling, or operating it. Current ordinary approval rows remain non-expandable when the composer owns the actionable approval.

Open activity detail owns its real pixel height. A shared disclosure motion controller mounts at `0px`, measures the nearest detail content from its bounding rectangle, and uses the Chromium `ResizeObserver` and Web Animations APIs as required platform capabilities. Opening, resizing, retargeting, reversal, and closing are all owned by one current animation; its `finished` promise is the only completion signal that may advance presence state or unmount content. CSS does not own a second height-transition clock, and animation completion is not inferred from elapsed timers. Consecutive content changes cancel the previous animation and redirect the current visual height to the latest measured target. Content growth and shrinkage use a 280 ms animation, including terminal polling, command expansion, responsive reflow, error replacement, and disappearing controls. The nearest detail disclosure owns this dynamic resize; the Env App timeline wrapper animates only its own open or close, preventing nested height animations from compounding movement. Detail content remains capped at `min(42rem, 72vh)`, and terminal output remains capped at five visual lines; growth beyond those limits becomes local scrolling instead of continuing to move the conversation.

Flower schedules its existing transcript-tail follower on every disclosure animation frame. It preserves the bottom anchor only while the user is already following latest content, and it never forces a conversation back to the end after the user scrolls upward. Env App continues to use `VirtualMessageList` row `ResizeObserver` handling: following mode applies each intermediate height delta, while paused mode restores the captured viewport anchor after every observed height. Terminal panels use a separate local 24 px near-bottom threshold. Binding or reopening a terminal viewport begins a new local viewing session at the latest line. Each output update receives one presentation frame: following mode scrolls to the bottom before reporting the output as presented, while paused mode preserves `scrollTop` and still reports presentation completion. Upward wheel or scroll pauses local following, returning to the bottom resumes it, and canonical activity replacement does not reset that choice. Output merging, polling cadence, canonical status ownership, and persistence are unchanged. Reduced-motion presentation suppresses routine automatic expansion, keeps attention and manual access immediate, commits measured pixel height without animation, and unmounts closed detail immediately. These presentation rules do not alter Floret activity status, event ordering, persistence, read state, audit state, or terminal settlement.

concepts.title: Flower timeline ordering
concepts.description: Canonical turn pages, live projections, decorations, cursor identity, and replacement events.

frontmatter.description: Canonical turn pages, live projections, decorations, cursor identity, and replacement events.
frontmatter.title: Flower timeline ordering

concepts.summary: Flower renders server-ordered Floret turn pages and validated live projections without client-side message sorting or reconstruction. Durable ordinal order, projection availability, draft identity, decorations, and replacement events remain explicit contracts. Live drafts may update only the matching canonical thread, turn, and run; resynchronization replaces the timeline rather than inventing assistant content or recovering from product audit rows.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The Flower live bootstrap includes `timeline_messages` as the only ordered message source for a thread snapshot. Redeven obtains before/after/tail pages from Floret `ListThreadTurns` and preserves their ascending canonical ordinal order. Each `ThreadTurnSnapshot` maps to the Floret `UserEntryID` user row followed by that same turn's validated assistant projection or one anchored `turn_projection_unavailable` decoration. Tool display details come from the projection `ActivityTimeline` plus Redeven's `ToolPresentationSpec`. A live draft may replace only the assistant slot whose `ThreadID`, `TurnID`, and `RunID` exactly match the canonical turn; terminal projection removes it. Redeven stores no transcript, correlation row, run record, projection cache, preview, or ordering copy.

Projection application is strict and monotonic. Floret `ThreadTurnProjection.Validate()` owns identity completeness, finite status and segment kinds, positive durable `ThroughOrdinal`, activity timeline validity, and control-signal structure. Redeven checks only that the validated projection belongs to the current Flower run/thread/turn, then maps assistant text, activity timelines with Redeven file actions, and non-body control segments. Live projections accept `running`; history assembly separately requires `Status.IsTerminal()`. Redeven keeps the last applied ordinal for each full execution identity, ignores equal or older projections, and advances the cursor only after the complete projection maps and replaces the assistant blocks. A valid zero-segment projection clears the in-memory assistant block list without synthesizing an empty markdown block. Runtime events are accepted only after Floret `runtime.Event.Validate()` validates their public event type and nested stream, activity, and projection data. Invalid contracts record `floret.contract.rejected` and leave the current presentation unchanged; private harness events never enter the public runtime sink.

Published Floret v0.11.4 includes the complete projection in each admitted public turn snapshot, provides a canonical latest-turn read that excludes marker-only turns until their user entry is committed, and reports projection availability separately for live run and pending-settlement results. Floret validators own availability combinations, identity, status, through ordinal, control signals, approvals, and settlement agreement. An invalid snapshot or projection is a canonical consistency error and requests resynchronization; Redeven does not turn it into display content. Only a valid projection with no renderable assistant blocks maps to one `turn_projection_unavailable` decoration with reason `not_renderable` immediately after that turn's user row. Flower renders “Response unavailable” with no retry control and no invented assistant body. Storage, I/O, pagination, unknown row cursors, and same-revision thread consistency failures remain request errors rather than being downgraded. Bootstrap, paged history, and `timeline.replaced` never recover content from metadata, product run errors, audit records, previews, or stale live drafts.

Timeline decorations are a strict discriminated union. `context_compaction` owns only a compaction payload, while `turn_projection_unavailable` owns only its projection-unavailable payload and finite reason. Unknown kinds, missing payloads, mixed payloads, invalid reason codes, or missing message anchors fail mapping explicitly. `ListThreadMessagesResponse`, live bootstrap, and full replacement snapshots carry `timeline_decorations` beside the messages produced by the same timeline projection, so a valid assistant projection and an unavailable decoration are mutually exclusive for one turn.

Live streaming uses server events only for current-process presentation. `message.started` may create one assistant streaming row and marks it as the single active cursor. There is no realtime transcript-message or transcript-reset event, and user messages are never inserted through the live stream. Floret text deltas may render temporary live markdown, while a validated `runtime.Event.Projection` with status `running` replaces only the matching live assistant draft with current assistant text and activity blocks. Aggregate Floret `runtime.Event.ActivityTimeline` remains lifecycle observation data and does not create main-thread activity blocks by itself. A local tool row is pending when Floret has observed the model's tool call but has not dispatched the handler; it becomes running only from Floret's dispatch-start lifecycle fact after permission and approval gates pass. Batched ordinary calls may start and finish in actual execution order, and approval display order never serializes handler execution. A projection that fails Floret validation or cannot be mapped is rejected as a unit and does not clear existing live content; a valid zero-segment projection clears the assistant blocks without inventing a body block. Stop and hard-cancel paths update current-process presentation but do not persist a canceled assistant row. Activity timeline changes enter the UI only inside Floret-derived projection activity segments delivered through `message.block_set`, a live draft, or a projection-backed timeline snapshot. Ordinary incremental events do not publish companion full timeline replacements. Every ordinary terminal turn and pending terminal settlement deletes the exact matching draft and publishes one `timeline.replaced` snapshot rebuilt from `ListThreadTurns`; an identity or ordinal mismatch requests resynchronization. `stream_generation` remains a Service-instance epoch so restart cannot let a stale high cursor hide new events.

Active cursor ownership belongs to Redeven's live projection. The projection marks at most one live assistant draft with `active_cursor` while the selected thread is running, and canceled, completed, idle, background, or merely streaming-shaped messages are not treated as active unless the projection marks them active. The visible model status affordance is a localized bottom dock lane driven by `model_io.updated`, not by `active_cursor` or thread running status. `model_io` is derived in memory from validated Floret provider lifecycle and stream observations: provider request maps to waiting for model response; assistant, reasoning, and model tool-call stream observations map to thinking; provider retry maps to retrying; provider finish maps to finalizing. Redeven does not persist Floret step, run, context-continuation, or compaction lifecycle mirrors. Provider diagnostics use typed finish fields; metadata cannot replace lifecycle facts.

Before Floret admission, Redeven may persist a pending command with a pre-generated opaque turn/run identity, prompt, product resource references, and launch options. No assistant draft is published at this stage. Floret admission creates the canonical user entry and turn; after `ListThreadTurns` confirms the exact `TurnID`, Redeven removes the pending prompt and retains only allowed product references. A crash before admission leaves the command pending, while a crash after admission is reconciled by exact Floret identity. Secret answers never enter plaintext persistence.

Selected-thread live polling treats `model_io.updated` as a presentation boundary. Even when one live-events response contains a short provider stream followed by finalizing or clearing events, Flower commits the model status lane and yields a render frame at each model I/O boundary. This keeps brief but real provider streaming phases visible to the user without inventing timeline rows, fallback timers, or thread-status heuristics.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Flower does not sort messages, infer canonical insertion points from timestamps or IDs, or pair recent user messages with assistant drafts. `timeline.replaced` replaces the reducer message array in server order. A streaming assistant row requires exact canonical thread/turn/run identity; an unmatched draft triggers resynchronization and is never appended. Local pending user rows are composer feedback for commands not yet admitted by Floret and never become durable history by themselves.

Flower does not rebuild Floret execution order from Redeven database rows, audit data, provider deltas, previews, or tool-call records. Floret `ListThreadTurns` is the durable message and ordering source; live runtime projections and pending settlement projections may update only the matching canonical turn. Flower does not scan cached block JSON or activity rows to repair pending, waiting, or running tool states after stop or approval. Redeven does not publish independent activity blocks from aggregate observations, synthesize final rows, hide queued siblings, or turn an invalid projection into an empty assistant block.

Redeven may publish `timeline.replaced` only as an explicit resync, snapshot recovery, or terminal settlement finalization event. It is not a high-frequency companion for streaming text, block updates, committed messages, run status changes, or thread patches. Desktop and Env App adapters both refresh the live bootstrap after thread-level stop operations, so the surface receives the same canonical timeline after a stop-only or stop+send interaction.

concepts.title: Flower turn launcher
concepts.description: Flower first-turn launchers collect scoped context and send one turn before handing off to the owning Flower surface.

frontmatter.description: Flower first-turn launchers collect scoped context and send one turn before handing off to the owning Flower surface.
frontmatter.title: Flower turn launcher

concepts.summary: Redeven uses a shared Flower turn launcher for contextual Ask Flower entrypoints. The launcher is a focused first-turn composer: it previews the captured context, accepts one prompt, calls the shared turn-launch contract, and then lets the host surface hand off to the correct full Flower chat destination.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Shared Flower contracts define `FlowerTurnLauncherIntent`, context items, attachments, and the shared `FlowerSurfaceAdapter` turn controls: `launchTurn` for sending and `stopThread` for canceling the selected running thread. `FlowerTurnLauncherWindow` renders the floating first-turn UI and exposes `onSubmit` instead of owning Desktop or Env App navigation. Its primary action uses the same circular primary ArrowUp button family as the full Flower composer. Click or Enter sets the launcher's `sending` state before invoking the host callback, immediately swaps the ArrowUp icon for the shared loading indicator, marks the control busy, and disables input, close, linked-context, and duplicate-submit actions until the callback settles. `FlowerSurface` uses the same `launchTurn` contract for normal chat sends, so new threads and continued conversations share one adapter path. The full Flower composer uses `stopThread` when the selected thread is running or waiting for approval: with an empty draft, the primary action becomes a stop icon and cancels the current run; with a non-empty draft, the action remains send-shaped and executes stop before launching the typed turn. Desktop Welcome opens the launcher from an environment card, builds the shared `assistant.ask.flower` context action, submits through the local environment adapter, creates a one-shot thread focus request for the returned thread id, and opens the Welcome Flower page. Env App captures its view-mode handoff context when the launcher opens; after a successful submit it creates the same one-shot AI thread focus request and routes to Activity or Workbench using the captured target. Gateway sessions preserve `runtime_gateway` as their execution context source instead of falling through to generic remote sources. A linked context preview must correspond to a valid `context_action`, including target, locality, source surface, and execution hint values accepted by the runtime validator; hosts must reject an explicit malformed context action instead of silently sending a context-free turn.

Ask Flower entrypoints deliberately separate linked context from file content sharing. File browser background/single/multi-selection menus, Codespaces directories, Activity and Workbench file previews, and future editor-preview launchers create `file_path` context items only; selecting text in a file preview does not create model-visible `file_selection` context and does not auto-create a pending text attachment. Terminal selection and session menus create one `terminal_selection`: short selected text remains inline, while long selections are linked by Unicode code-point length/truncation metadata only and do not become implicit file attachments. Monitoring process menus create `process_snapshot` metadata, Git Changes/branch/commit surfaces create bounded generated `text_snapshot` summaries, and Desktop Welcome creates environment metadata snapshots with the same fallback title used by the launcher. `editor_preview` remains a protocol-compatible file-path source without a producer.

Pending attachments remain an explicit launcher transport feature and are not converted into file-path linked context. Env App must upload every pending file before sending; a missing uploader or upload failure blocks the turn with an error. Desktop Welcome currently has no upload handler and rejects pending files explicitly. The model-facing Flower turn receives only attachment labels and MIME metadata through the backend Floret supplemental-context formatter; upload URLs and file bodies are excluded from `Input`, `SupplementalContext`, and context-action events.

Immediate optimistic messages retain their context action. Thread detail also returns canonical queued-turn records, allowing refresh, Activity/Workbench handoff, and service restart to hydrate pending prompt, message id, creation time, and linked context. `queued_turns` is an owned detail field: omission in a partial summary preserves loaded detail, an explicit empty list clears it, and a nonempty list replaces it. When timeline replacement contains the canonical user message with the same message id, Flower removes the pending projection so the linked-context chip appears once across success, failure, and cancellation states.

Transcript linked-context actions distinguish host navigation from self-contained snapshot preview. In Env App, file chips delegate their canonical path to the existing live File Preview host and directory chips delegate to the existing Files host, so Activity, Workbench, and mobile placement remain owned by the standard environment controllers. The shared Flower floating preview accepts only text and process snapshot actions, including short terminal selections, historical display-only file selections, and generated Git or environment snapshots; it must never render a placeholder file or directory window. A host exposes file and directory navigation independently through optional adapter capabilities. Desktop Welcome does not expose those capabilities, so file and directory records from shared local history remain visible but noninteractive there. Capability absence and host failures never fall back to an explanatory or empty floating window.

Flower thread working directory is a Redeven product thread attribute decided at creation time. `FlowerSurfaceAdapter` exposes optional working-directory picker reads for path context and directory entries; Env App implements them through the existing runtime FS RPC, while Desktop Welcome implements them through the fixed runtime FS Local API bridge. In New chat, `FlowerSurface` keeps a local `workingDirDraft`, displays only the basename in the header chip, opens the shared `DirectoryPicker` when the chip is clicked, and sends `working_dir` only when creating a new thread. Existing threads display their immutable `thread.working_dir` basename with the full path in title/ARIA text; clicking that chip copies the full path and never opens a picker or patches the thread.

Env App implements `stopThread` with the existing AI RPC stop endpoint and then reloads the thread bootstrap. Desktop implements the same adapter method through the runtime Flower HTTP bridge, which allows `POST /_redeven_proxy/api/ai/threads/:id/cancel` and reloads `/live/bootstrap`. The app server thread cancel route preserves RWX permission checks and audit events while calling `StopThread`, so queued followups are recovered to drafts before the active run is canceled.

Stop cancellation is UI-responsive but engine-admission guarded. When Redeven detaches an active run for Stop, it removes the active-run mapping so the UI can leave the running state, then keeps an in-memory per-thread stop-finalizing guard until the detached run's `doneCh` closes after terminal cleanup and Floret turn finalization. New user input during that window is accepted as a queued followup, but queued drain and direct detached-run start paths must not start another Floret turn until the guard clears. A Redeven-requested cancel or timeout that returns from Floret as a context cancellation is projected as canceled or timed out, not as `floret_engine_failed`.

Environment cards can expose placement-shaped targets such as local container, SSH, provider, or Gateway targets. These identifiers are routing context for Redeven, not permission to infer Docker, SSH, systemd, or Gateway control commands. Flower must interpret environment lifecycle requests through the `redeven-environment` system skill and the `redeven env ... --json` surface so unsupported target kinds return structured product plans instead of lower-level command guesses. For arbitrary OS diagnostics on a selected target, the same skill routes Flower to `redeven targets exec --target <target> --command <agent-selected command> --json`, where Redeven owns target resolution and execution provenance.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
The launcher is not a persistent chat surface and does not inject drafts into the full Flower composer. Hosts must not navigate to Flower before a successful `launchTurn`, must not require a second send in the full chat, and must not re-read the current Env mode at submit time to decide the handoff target. A failed launcher submit clears the busy state, preserves the visible draft, restores the ArrowUp action, presents the host error, and returns focus to the textarea; successful hosts may close the launcher as part of their existing thread handoff. The launcher does not add an artificial minimum loading delay. Composer stop+send is not queueing at the UI boundary: the current run is stopped first, and only then is the visible draft submitted through `launchTurn`; if stopping or sending fails, the typed draft remains in the composer. Runtime admission may still queue the submitted turn until stop finalization closes the previous provider-visible ledger. Redeven must not read Floret sqlite, synthesize Floret tool results from audit rows, or treat terminal audit cancellation as a substitute for Floret durable tool-call/tool-result closure. `waiting_user` threads stay on the structured input Continue flow and do not enter the stop/stop+send matrix. Focus handoff is a consumed request, not persistent selected-thread state; after `FlowerSurface` accepts a request or the user selects another thread, the host clears that request so remounts and list refreshes cannot pull the user back to the handoff thread. Thread working directories are not mutable after creation: appserver thread patch decoding rejects unknown fields such as `working_dir`, and an empty create request falls back to the runtime `agentHomeDir` before validation. Context actions are product records rather than UI-only metadata: the runtime validates the standard Ask Flower action before accepting the turn, persists the normalized envelope with the transcript message, and projects the accepted record into Floret only as current-turn supplemental context. File-related context is path/metadata-only, and upload URLs or file bodies must not be displayed as implied model context in launcher copy. The target context does not alter builtin tool execution. In particular, `terminal.exec` still runs in the local AI runtime; only Redeven product command results or target-routed tool results with explicit `execution_location` can justify saying a command ran on a selected target. The full Flower transcript parses context actions in persisted-display mode: it may recover only deterministic historical schema v2 omissions, may show historical file selections without resubmission, and renders unknown or damaged individual items as noninteractive unsupported chips. New launcher submissions use strict-input mode and never inherit those history-only allowances.

A persisted file path is display and navigation metadata, not a filesystem read grant. After a user click, the Env App host normalizes the path and delegates to its existing preview or directory surface, where session and filesystem read policy remains authoritative. That host read does not mutate the context action, attach file contents to the transcript, or create new model-visible supplemental context. Desktop and any future host without the matching capability must render the reference without a click affordance instead of inventing a fallback interaction.

concepts.title: Plugin surfaces
concepts.description: Redeven places official ReDevPlugin sandbox surfaces into Env App chrome without making Plugin Center a Settings page or overlay.

frontmatter.description: Redeven places official ReDevPlugin sandbox surfaces into Env App chrome without making Plugin Center a Settings page or overlay.
frontmatter.title: Plugin surfaces

concepts.summary:
Plugin surfaces are ReDevPlugin sandbox documents placed inside Redeven product
chrome. Redeven decides discovery, placement, and product navigation, while
ReDevPlugin remains responsible for plugin identity, lifecycle, permissions,
confirmation, asset tickets, bridge tokens, RPC, streams, leases, audit, and
registry state.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Env App source contains a two-level official plugin entry, but product discovery
is development-only until the feature is ready for users. Vite serve enables the
entry automatically. Production-style builds enable it only when the owning
development launcher sets `REDEVEN_ENVAPP_ENABLE_PLUGIN_UI=1`; ordinary builds,
CI builds, and releases fail closed. A disabled build does not add the Activity
Bar `Plugins` item, register the internal Plugin Center or Plugin Surface
Activities, mount the panel, or start the plugin inventory resource.

When enabled, the Activity Bar `Plugins` item opens an app-grid panel without
changing the current normal Activity or Workbench surface. The first panel tile
is always `Plugin Center`. Enabled official plugins with a default launch target
open a sandbox plugin surface; unavailable, disabled, attention-needed, or
not-installed plugins route to Plugin Center details instead of pretending that
an iframe opened.

Plugin Center is a dedicated Env App management Activity, not Runtime Settings,
not a modal, and not a floating overlay. Opening it activates the internal
`plugin-center` Activity id and renders `PluginCenterView` in the Floe shell
main slot. Closing it returns to the last normal Activity surface, with
`terminal` as the default return target. Runtime Settings remains free of a
plugins section.

Official discovery is limited to the bundled Redeven official catalog seed.
The current seed contains `com.redeven.official.containers`. Plugin Center
merges that official catalog item with matching installed ReDevPlugin records
by `plugin_id`, excludes non-official installed records from this management
surface, and treats non-runnable trust states as needs-attention even when the
plugin id is official. First-release UX deliberately omits third-party
marketplace, developer mode, URL install, file install, unsigned local install,
ratings, comments, and remote search.

Official install and update use a bundled official package. Env App embeds the
Containers `.redevplugin` package as `officialPluginPackages.ts`; the UI sends
`package_base64` with `trust_state: "bundled"` to
`/_redeven_proxy/api/plugins/install` or `/_redeven_proxy/api/plugins/update`.
The UI does not download package URLs, verify signatures, parse manifests, or
write registry state. It delegates package validation, staging, trust-state
assignment, lifecycle mutation, retained data, and audit to the mounted
ReDevPlugin handler.

Enabled official plugin surfaces open through the released ReDevPlugin surface
open endpoint under `/_redeven_proxy/api/plugins/surfaces/open`. Env App then
renders the internal `plugin-surface` Activity using `PluginSurfaceFrame`, a
thin Redeven placement wrapper over the published
`@floegence/redevplugin-ui@0.1.5` `PluginSurfaceHost`. The frame requests
asset bootstrap from the plugin sandbox origin under
`/_redeven_plugin/bootstrap`, loads the iframe document from
`/_redeven_plugin/assets/{asset_session_id}/ui/index.html`, and adapts SDK
management calls from `/_redevplugin/api/plugins*` to
`/_redeven_proxy/api/plugins*`. The published SDK remains responsible for
exact-origin bridge filtering, bridge handshake validation, bridge-token
requests, RPC forwarding, confirmation handling, and lifecycle disposal.

`PluginSurfaceFrame` must stay narrow and product-placement oriented. It may
connect released ReDevPlugin routes to Env App chrome, choose the correct
regional or local loopback plugin sandbox origin, and project SDK errors into
the Activity frame. It must not grow into a copied SDK, generated client,
registry, manifest parser, storage broker, stream broker, or runtime platform.

Workbench placement remains shell ownership. If a future plugin surface is
placed in Workbench, the wrapper must preserve Workbench wheel, text selection,
copy, action-control, and activation contracts through the existing exported
interaction markers. A native Redeven component that does not exercise the
ReDevPlugin sandbox bootstrap and bridge lifecycle is a Redeven feature, not an
official plugin surface.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Redeven UI state is a projection and command surface, not a plugin registry or
lifecycle source of truth. Install, enable, open, disable, uninstall, update,
diagnostics, export/import, and data-retention actions must call mounted
ReDevPlugin lifecycle APIs or released generated clients. The UI must not
compute CSRF tokens; AppServer and the ReDevPlugin integration wrapper attach
authoritative session headers before delegating management requests.

The build-time UI gate controls product discovery, not platform authorization.
Released backend integration, route isolation, runtime supervision, and business
adapters remain mounted and governed by ReDevPlugin policy while the production
Env App omits every user entry. The gate is not a runtime setting, URL option,
local-storage preference, or permission override.

Plugin assets and plugin RPC must not be smuggled through Env App dist,
codespace injection, port forwarding, direct Local UI session artifacts,
Gateway credentials, runtime-control tokens, or Flower grants. Plugin sandbox
documents load only through the plugin sandbox route family, and management
actions are Env App management routes only after Env App origin and Local UI
access gates have passed.

Official Containers is a sandboxed plugin surface over the Redeven
`container_resources` business capability. Containers are not a plugin runtime
mechanism and must not be used to execute third-party plugin backends.

concepts.snippet: Plugin surfaces are ReDevPlugin sandbox documents placed inside Redeven product chrome. Redeven decides discovery, placement, and product navigation, while ReDevPlugin remains responsible for plugin identity, lifecycle, permissions,

concepts.title: UI presentation transactions
concepts.description: Heavy selection changes present visual intent first, commit content after paint, and defer layout effects until content presentation.

frontmatter.description: Heavy selection changes present visual intent first, commit content after paint, and defer layout effects until content presentation.
frontmatter.title: UI presentation transactions

concepts.summary: Redeven prioritizes visible input acknowledgement over the secondary work caused by a selection change. A heavy navigation event is one presentation transaction with three ordered stages: the lightweight selected control is updated synchronously, target content visibility is committed after that intent can paint, and visibility-dependent focus, resize, scroll restoration, viewport movement, or measurement runs only after the target content can paint. This ordering applies to Activity navigation, Activity/Workbench mode, terminal sessions, Workbench widgets, Flower and Codex threads, Files/Git modes, and heavy Settings, Plugin Center, or Debug Console panels.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

The published floe-webapp controller exposes separate visual and committed values. User pointer, click, and keyboard paths call `request()` so visual state changes immediately and only the latest sequence reaches commit. Terminal may call `preview()` on pointer down before click and resets obsolete previews. Initialization, persisted restoration, permission correction, automatic open, and connection recovery use the synchronous canonical path such as `commitNow()` because they are not waiting to acknowledge a user input. External canonical state changes cancel an older pending transaction and synchronize the visual target. Cleanup cancels scheduled work so a disposed owner cannot commit later.

The standard event sequence is `requested`, `intent_presented`, `commit_started`, `committed`, and `content_presented`. `cancelled` terminates a transaction that loses ownership or is disposed. A monotonically increasing transaction or request identity suppresses stale commits: rapid input may update visual state more than once, but only the newest target may mount a cold feature, fetch thread detail, restore focus, resize a terminal, move the Workbench viewport, or publish content presentation. Persistence follows the committed state and runs once; a preview is never persisted as canonical state.

Keep-alive and UI-first scheduling are complementary. Once a surface, provider, terminal core, transcript, or heavy panel has been visited, ordinary navigation keeps its DOM and runtime identity alive while changing visibility. Hidden surfaces continue receiving terminal output, monitor updates, thread events, provider state, drafts, subscriptions, and other data flow. Navigation must not trigger cleanup, suspend, hibernate, refetch, history replay, provider initialization, or resource degradation. A legitimate first lazy visit may show loading inside the target feature, but the selected navigation control still presents first and the loading state must not cover the ActivityBar or replace the previous warm transcript during staged thread loading.

Resource collections follow the same stable-presentation rule. A first Codespaces or Web Services request may show a card-shaped skeleton only after a 150 ms quiet period, using the collection surface rather than a contrasting blocking curtain. Once a collection has resolved, background refresh keeps the rendered cards or empty state, filters, and local scroll position mounted until the replacement snapshot is ready; only the initiating refresh control and accessibility status report the in-flight work. Operations that genuinely block the surface, such as resolving and opening a Web Service route, retain their dedicated blocking presentation.

Activity uses the Shell UI-first mode and after-paint `ActivityAppsMain` activation. Activity and Workbench are also two kept-alive views with after-paint activation. Files and Git mount on first visit and retain path, filters, selection, view mode, expanded state, and scroll. Terminal warm cores become visible without replay or reconstruction; focus and resize follow content presentation. Workbench widget selection updates the selected boundary before z-order persistence, viewport motion, fit, focus, and measurement. Flower and Codex keep an old transcript visible while a cold target loads and atomically replace it only when the latest target is ready.

Debug Console subscribes to the product presentation event stream and groups samples by `surface + source`. It records intent-paint, commit, and content-paint p50, p95, and maximum values. Intent paint targets p95 at or below 16.7 ms, with a hard acceptance ceiling of 32 ms. A user interaction must not produce a 50 ms or longer renderer task or frame gap. Warm content presentation targets p95 at or below 50 ms and maximum at or below 100 ms. First lazy visits retain the 32 ms visual-feedback ceiling even when target-local loading takes longer. Debug instrumentation reports a slow presentation when intent reaches 32 ms or content reaches 100 ms; performance gates and browser tests provide the stricter distribution and long-task assertions.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
UI-first scheduling is not permission to duplicate canonical state or create a second business model. Visual state is presentation-only, committed state remains the source for persistence and business effects, and external canonical corrections win. The transaction layer does not change backend protocols, persistent schemas, permission semantics, terminal transport, thread ordering, or provider ownership.

Not every interaction should wait one frame. A control that only changes lightweight local presentation should remain synchronous. The transaction contract is required when one input would otherwise couple selected feedback to heavy DOM visibility, lazy mount, RPC or history work, focus, resize, scroll, viewport animation, layout measurement, or persistence.

Keep-alive does not mean eagerly loading every feature. Initial resources still exclude heavy feature modules and styles, and an unvisited surface remains lazy. After first activation, however, ordinary navigation cannot convert that surface back into an unloaded state merely to reduce background work. Explicit environment replacement, permission-root replacement, connection recovery, resource-pressure policy already owned by the relevant subsystem, and user-requested closure may establish separate lifecycle boundaries; a navigation click alone may not.

concepts.title: Workbench input ownership
concepts.description: Wheel, pointer, local-scroll, and text-selection ownership.

frontmatter.description: Wheel, pointer, local-scroll, and text-selection ownership.
frontmatter.title: Workbench input ownership

concepts.summary: Workbench input ownership is declared through explicit marker contracts so canvas zoom, local scrolling, pointer actions, and text selection remain distinguishable. A selected widget boundary does not automatically own wheel input, and local-scroll regions must declare their role rather than relying on incidental DOM structure. These contracts let the canvas remain predictable while nested product surfaces preserve native interaction.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Wheel ownership uses exported local scroll viewport props that set `data-floe-canvas-wheel-interactive="true"` and a local-scroll role. Layout-only regions can declare a non-interactive wheel role. Text selection surfaces use separate marker props and do not receive wheel ownership unless combined with local scroll viewport props. Action surfaces declare their own marker so clickable controls can remain interactive inside widgets without becoming text selection surfaces. Terminal keyboard shortcuts such as panel-local search and session switching are scoped to the owning TerminalPanel event tree; they do not create a window-level shortcut owner and do not widen wheel, selection, copy, or widget activation ownership. The published Floeterm terminal core portals the Ghostty textarea to the owning document body and derives its fixed `left`, `top`, `width`, `height`, and line height from active terminal geometry plus the canvas's visible viewport scale. Redeven supplies placement and focus policy but does not override the textarea position, reset terminal-host scrolling after focus, resize the PTY to compensate for projection, or add a Workbench-only input path. Terminal session selection uses the shared UI-first selection controller and a Floe Sidebar session list. Pointer down may preview the target, while click and keyboard selection request the same transaction: the active sidebar item reaches the screen first, and the visible Runtime mounts in the same committed selection update without a second paint wait. This path does not wait for parent Workbench state propagation, terminal core mount, history replay, focus restoration, RPCs, disposal work, or Workbench viewport navigation. The desktop sidebar and mobile drawer share `TerminalSessionNavigator`, while terminal text search stays in a surface-local overlay and per-session Core/output behavior stays in `TerminalSessionRuntime`; these components consume the panel's existing state and do not establish parallel lifecycle ownership. Mounted terminal sessions remain warm across ordinary sidebar switches: the released Floeterm paged output coordinator retains inactive live output, catches up sparse sequence gaps, and cancels obsolete recovery work. Returning to an already mounted session must not trigger blocking reload, core disposal, attach, full history replay, or widget centering. Automatic background recovery does not block input; only initial attach and replay do. Retry-wait and failed recovery are visible, and exhausted recovery provides a manual retry action. Terminal sidebar items are directory-first: their visible title and circular initial derive from the session working directory, while their running badge reflects the session work state and takes priority over unread decoration. Terminal sidebar path actions are separate panel-local controls; activity mode opens the floating file browser and workbench mode requests a new Files canvas surface through the existing file-browser handoff. Terminal session creation, first activation of an unmounted terminal session, and close interactions are UI-first inside the owning panel: sidebar selection or removal reaches the screen before terminal core mount, initial history replay, delete RPCs, or disposal work begins. Closing a Workbench terminal widget schedules Workbench-scoped cleanup for the sessions currently bound to that widget, but widget removal remains a fire-and-forget UI transition and does not wait for PTY disposal.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body: Selected widget boundaries guard canvas zoom. A surface that supports text selection does not automatically own wheel input, and a surface that owns local scrolling must declare the exported wheel contract props. Terminal shortcuts may switch only the current panel's visible sidebar sessions and must preserve platform primary-mod behavior so terminal-native Ctrl sequences are not stolen on macOS. Terminal sidebar path actions are file-browser handoffs only; they must not select the terminal session, bypass read permission checks, create a global file-browser command, or change wheel, text-selection, copy, shortcut, terminal transport, or widget activation ownership. Terminal UI-first scheduling is a responsiveness contract only; it must not pre-mount hidden terminal sessions, pause background output, move terminal shortcuts to a global listener, restore the top tab strip or measured slider animation for terminal session selection, or change terminal transport semantics. A selection transaction superseded before its committed update is cancelled; once committed, the visible target Runtime mounts in that same update without a second paint delay. Rapid A-to-B-to-C selection therefore mounts only the final committed target instead of attaching every briefly previewed row. Activity and Workbench output recovery use the same released Floeterm coordinator. Redeven must not add a second queue, catch-up state machine, snapshot format, payload projection cache, or sequence compatibility shim. Sparse history uses the explicit `coveredThroughSequence` field as its coverage boundary even when some covered source sequences have no display chunk; `lastSequence`, retained chunks, and page emptiness are never coverage substitutes. Valid zero coverage is distinct from a missing or malformed contract. Live output omitted from a sparse history page must still render exactly once, while Shell/OSC projection side effects must run only in the accepted ordered transform path and must not repeat. Clear, remount, session switch, and disposal cancel obsolete coordinator work. Before an inactive renderer is released, coordinator pause must await the in-flight writer and its coverage commit; after pause returns, the cancelled recovery may not start another history batch. Initialization autofocus may restore focus only if initialization began with focus empty or inside the same terminal and completion still belongs to the same surface generation. Session-selection focus is a separate request-time intent: it may retry after baseline only while its generation and committed session remain current, the view and keyboard policy still allow native focus, and focus remains at the original sidebar anchor, body, origin terminal, or target terminal. Search, settings, footer actions, text selections, other controls, and other widgets cancel that intent and retain focus. The terminal footer reserves 28 px from the first desktop frame and 44 px while the Floe mobile keyboard is visible; both modes keep stable tracks, overflow containment, and fixed action targets so recovery text and actions never change xterm rows or overlap the keyboard. Resource-pressure hibernation is scheduled only as idle work, never selects the current core, and defers cores with input, IME composition, selection, search, or context-menu interaction. Hibernation releases only renderer resources: the PTY, coordinator, output subscription, activity state, and session lifecycle continue. Snapshot memory uses a separate LRU budget and is never persisted; an absent, incompatible, damaged, or evicted snapshot falls back to paged history without leaving a blank terminal state. On mobile, the session list is a Workbench-local drawer over a full-width terminal surface. Search, outside click, Escape, close, selection, and browser history dismissal remain panel-local, and selecting a row, including the already-active row, closes the drawer before any policy-allowed terminal focus restoration. Env App Workbench surface activation keeps `ensureVisible` and `centerViewport` separate: `ensureVisible` asks the Workbench page to reveal a target only when it is not already projected into the current viewport, while explicit `centerViewport: false` and anchored creation preserve the current viewport. Terminal input focus restoration is separate from Workbench viewport navigation, so focusing an already visible terminal widget must not start a viewport-centering animation or mutate the terminal surface scroll offset as a visual fallback. Native components embedded in projected Workbench surfaces must not hand-roll viewport-to-surface coordinate conversion for floating UI; using the shared floating layer is the contract that keeps standalone and projected placements equivalent. Tests for Workbench-capable floating UI must exercise projected or transformed surface placement and assert shared local interaction layer hosting rather than only checking ordinary page rendering.

frontmatter.title: Workbench interaction contracts

concepts.summary: Workbench interaction is governed by explicit ownership contracts rather than widget-specific event heuristics. This overview is the canonical navigation point for input ownership and surface lifecycle. Focused concepts define wheel, pointer, text, terminal, selection, recovery, lazy loading, and shared overlay behavior without mixing those independent responsibilities into one retrieval unit.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

This concept is the stable overview for the subject. Detailed contracts are maintained in the focused concepts below:

- [Workbench input ownership](workbench-input-ownership.md)
- [Workbench terminal interaction](workbench-terminal-interaction.md)

- [Workbench surface lifecycle](workbench-surface-lifecycle.md)

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body: Workbench widgets must not infer canvas, input, recovery, focus, or overlay ownership from incidental DOM structure. Published Floeterm and floe-webapp contracts remain upstream authorities for their generic behavior.

concepts.title: Workbench surface lifecycle
concepts.description: Selection presentation, recovery ownership, lazy widgets, and shared floating surfaces.

frontmatter.description: Selection presentation, recovery ownership, lazy widgets, and shared floating surfaces.
frontmatter.title: Workbench surface lifecycle

concepts.summary: Workbench keeps widget identity and state stable while visual selection, committed activation, lazy feature loading, and connection recovery follow explicit presentation ownership. Recovery is mounted above the canvas and makes retained content inert instead of creating per-widget reconnect state. Pointer-anchored overlays use the shared surface layer, and lazy module delivery cannot replace permission, keep-alive, or lifecycle contracts.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body:
## Mechanism

Workbench widget selection follows the same presentation ordering without changing canvas ownership. The selected boundary and pointer ownership update immediately; committed activation, z-order persistence, viewport reveal or centering, fit, focus, and geometry measurement occur after the intent paint. Activity and Workbench page roots also remain mounted after first visit. Switching page mode changes visibility and activation sequence after paint, then restores Workbench geometry and focus; it does not rebuild the Workbench page or destroy the Activity Shell.

Unexpected connection recovery is owned above the Workbench canvas. `ConnectionRecoveryView` fills the Workbench content root while the mounted canvas remains the same KeepAlive instance underneath. During recovery the canvas container is `inert`, pointer-disabled, and hidden from assistive technology, so selected widgets, terminals, floating layers, keyboard handlers, and canvas gestures cannot act on stale Runtime state. The recovery view is ordinary shell content rather than a projected widget, nested card, or Workbench floating layer. When the shared recovery snapshot returns to idle after its success hold, the existing viewport, widget selection, canvas state, and mounted feature bodies resume without reconstruction.

A Desktop transport snapshot in `ready` phase must still belong to the exact registered bridge session whose stable loopback proxy remains alive. Desktop health consumers are read-only observers: they may expose `recovering` while the bridge is reconnecting or `unavailable` after a typed Local UI probe failure, but neither result may retire the bridge or strand the retained shell on a stale `ready` record. Only the bridge session may advance recovery phases or close automatically. When that same session returns to `ready`, Env App resumes protocol and secure-session recovery through the original loopback URL and mounted Workbench tree; terminal bridge failure keeps the tree inert until explicit Open creates a new Desktop session.

Workbench widget definitions may lazy-load their feature body, including Terminal, Files, Monitor, Codespaces, Ports, Flower, and Codex. The widget identity, persisted instance state, selected state, activation sequence, permission fallback, and viewport policy exist outside the feature module and must be forwarded unchanged when the chunk resolves. Lazy resolution may delay the first body mount, but it must not create a second widget instance, reset persisted state, bypass RWX permission notices, or convert activation into an implicit viewport-centering request. Tests that inspect lazy widget bodies wait for module resolution before asserting the live component contract.

Projected Workbench surfaces must delegate pointer-anchored overlays to the shared surface floating layer. Menus opened from right-click, menu buttons, or keyboard anchors should pass client or anchor coordinates to `SurfaceFloatingLayer`, which owns surface-local projection, clamping, z-index, and local interaction markers. Context menus, dropdowns, popovers, hover cards, tooltips, autocomplete panels, command palettes, color pickers, date pickers, and equivalent floating UI must not treat Workbench as ordinary document flow. Their panel content may own role, focus, keyboard navigation, item layout, and visual styling, but must not own `position: fixed`, inline viewport `left` / `top`, `window.innerWidth` / `window.innerHeight` clamping, body portals, or component-local viewport-to-surface coordinate conversion inside a transformed projected surface.

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Lazy loading is a module-delivery boundary only. It must not pre-mount inactive Workbench features, eagerly initialize Flower or Codex providers, or weaken existing permission, state restoration, input ownership, and error recovery contracts.

Connection recovery must not be implemented independently inside Workbench widgets or feature bodies. Per-widget curtains, fallback controls, and local reconnect state would create conflicting interaction owners and allow stale controls to remain reachable. Only the shell-level recovery snapshot may suspend the Workbench interaction tree, and a terminal failure does not grant the retained canvas any read or write interaction until a new Desktop session is opened.

frontmatter.description: Terminal attachment, input-plane ownership, focus, retained history, and performance validation.
frontmatter.title: Workbench terminal interaction

concepts.summary: Workbench Terminal separates Runtime attachment, retained history, input-plane ownership, native focus, and fixed performance validation. Only the matching coordinator generation may complete attachment, and published Floeterm remains the authority for terminal input behavior. Mobile keyboard retention does not imply textarea autofocus, while performance evidence remains a dedicated Chromium and carrier contract rather than an ordinary CI timing claim.

sections.title: Contract
sections.body: ## Mechanism

sections.title: Boundaries
sections.body:
Terminal initial attachment is two-phase: live retention begins before the Runtime request, and only the matching coordinator generation may complete with the Runtime's atomic history boundary. Env App also assigns a separate monotonic attachment generation owned by the underlying protocol transport, so Activity and Workbench RPC facades share one order while the stream is shared. Runtime rejects stale generations before boundary capture, preserves failed-generation high-water until sink or session teardown, and cannot revive a closed sink or let stale activation rollback remove a newer attachment. A superseded interactive surface retries only while it still owns the view, active session, and focus intent; a background surface waits until it regains ownership, preventing both blank current terminals and cross-surface attach loops. Missing or malformed boundaries fail closed; a valid zero boundary completes without an unbounded history request. This contract applies equally to Activity and Workbench terminals and does not create a Redeven-owned recovery queue. Before the first Terminal surface is interactive, the Env catalog may idle-preload the Terminal feature chunk and published renderer resources. The Activity Terminal page does not mount `TerminalPanel` before the first successful catalog hydration. A short disconnect may mark an already hydrated snapshot stale and keep it mounted, but an initial refresh failure cannot use `stale` as a substitute for a snapshot. Explicit process-launch denial mounts the panel permission state instead of leaving the page gated. A server-side process denial is retained against the matching environment, protocol client, and permission snapshot so an automatic retry cannot hide the permission state; a new permission snapshot or client identity clears that fence and retries hydration. A catalog wait remains visually quiet for 150 ms, then shows a loading curtain; a catalog error shows a retry action. After an interactive Runtime is ready, an Env-scoped queue may prepare renderer-neutral history only for `isActive` sessions, one page and one session at a time, under a byte-bounded MRU cache. Each preparation is capped by the candidate's actual remaining-or-evictable byte budget, and a budget stop cannot cancel a more recent background candidate or any interactive request. A zero-byte incomplete seed is not cached because it cannot reduce interactive recovery work, while a complete empty-history seed remains valid. Prepared seeds are consumed through Floeterm's generation/retention validation; a mismatch falls back to normal paged recovery. If an unrelated background history RPC cannot be cancelled promptly, an interactive request abandons its seed attempt and uses normal recovery instead of waiting behind background work. Dormant sessions remain metadata-only: background work never creates a Core, attaches, resizes, subscribes to live output, or starts a PTY. Disconnect, page hiding, clear, delete, environment changes, and permission loss cancel or invalidate warmup without allowing late results to repopulate a newer connection epoch.

Terminal input-plane ownership is upstream-only. Redeven consumes the released Floeterm behavior and verifies it through a real Chromium Workbench flow with the actual `TerminalCore`, fit geometry, a preserved selected widget at 45% viewport scale, and Ghostty's canvas click path. The regression gate requires the textarea to stay in the document viewport plane, align with the visible cursor cell, leave render-host extents equal to client extents, preserve zero host scroll before and after focus, and keep canvas offsets and intrinsic/client dimensions unchanged. Context-menu transient placement, IME composition, and terminal-local shortcut ownership remain Floeterm contracts rather than Redeven compatibility branches.

Terminal attachment ownership is deliberately separate from native textarea autofocus. This lets a Floe mobile-keyboard surface retain and retry the real Runtime attachment without opening the system IME. Surface disposal invalidates reload work at asynchronous boundaries and queued retries, so a removed Activity or Workbench panel cannot perform a ghost attach or reclaim attachment ownership later.

Fixed-spec Terminal performance validation is separate from ordinary CI timing. Browser interaction samples calculate nearest-rank p95 for the preloaded Activity intent-to-real-sidebar path, hydrated sidebar presentation, pending-row paint, and warm-core switching. The Runtime/PTY carrier aggregates repeated 64 KiB shared prepared-history samples, uses a unique input marker and same-sample Activity canvas baseline for every run, and enforces both per-sample and p95 recovery limits. Equal terminal geometries use the strict normalized-grid comparison; different Activity and Workbench geometries combine generation, coverage, delta, and attach invariants with nonblank coarse canvas evidence instead of treating valid reflow as content loss. The unified report includes raw samples, a recomputed p95, tracked and untracked source revision state, runner identity, and carrier evidence. Absolute thresholds are enabled only by the fixed-performance runner; ordinary hosted CI retains deterministic frame, call-count, bundle-graph, coverage, and no-error gates.
